q: In the genetic code, what does a codon represent?
options: A three-nucleotide mRNA sequence that specifies one amino acid
A three-nucleotide tRNA sequence that base-pairs with mRNA
The three-nucleotide DNA start signal recognized by RNA polymerase
A single nucleotide that determines one amino acid in a protein
explain: A codon is a run of three nucleotides in mRNA that codes for one amino acid; the complementary tRNA triplet is the anticodon, not the codon, and the code is a triplet (not single-nucleotide) code.
hint: Count the nucleotides and note which molecule carries it and what it encodes.

q: Which part of an mRNA is the protein-coding region?
options: The Shine-Dalgarno sequence that recruits the ribosome
The poly(A) tail added to the 3' end during processing
The portion whose nucleotides specify the amino acid sequence of the protein
The 3' UTR, which controls transcript stability after the stop codon
explain: The protein-coding region runs from the start codon to the stop codon and its nucleotides specify the amino acid sequence; the UTRs, poly(A) tail, and Shine-Dalgarno site are untranslated even though they are part of the message.
hint: Only one segment is actually read codon-by-codon into amino acids.

q: The genetic code is described as _____, meaning that some amino acids are specified by more than one codon.
options: overlapping
degenerate
nonuniversal
ambiguous
explain: The code is degenerate (redundant): most amino acids are specified by several synonymous codons. 'Ambiguous' would mean one codon specifies more than one amino acid, which is not the case; 'overlapping' would mean codons share bases, which the code does not; 'nonuniversal' addresses whether the code is shared across organisms, not multiplicity of codons per amino acid.
hint: Think about what it means for multiple codons to map to a single amino acid versus a single codon mapping to multiple amino acids.

q: In the standard genetic code, most amino acids are specified by two or more synonymous codons. Which of the following amino acids is specified by only a SINGLE codon (i.e., NOT the product of synonymous codons)?
options: Methionine (Met)
Serine (Ser)
Arginine (Arg)
Leucine (Leu)
explain: Methionine is encoded by the single codon AUG (which also serves as the start codon); tryptophan (UGG) is the only other single-codon amino acid. Leucine, serine, and arginine are each specified by six synonymous codons.
hint: One of these doubles as the start codon and is encoded by AUG alone; the others each have several codons.

q: What experimental strategy gave Nirenberg and Matthaei their initial success in deciphering the genetic code?
options: Sequencing the DNA of bacterial genes and reading off codons directly
Generating synthetic RNA homopolymers and determining which amino acid was incorporated into protein
X-ray crystallography of the ribosome to map codon positions
Isolating natural mRNAs and measuring their poly(A) tail lengths
explain: Nirenberg and Matthaei added synthetic homopolymers (e.g., poly-U) to cell-free systems and saw which amino acid was incorporated (poly-U -> polyphenylalanine), assigning UUU = Phe. DNA sequencing and ribosome crystallography were not available as decoding tools at that time.
hint: Their famous first result came from a message made of a single repeated nucleotide (poly-U).

q: Which of the following statements about the characteristics of the genetic code is FALSE?
options: The code is read in nonoverlapping, consecutive triplets
The code is degenerate, with most amino acids specified by more than one codon
The code is nearly universal across organisms
The reading frame is set by an initiation codon, which does not code for an amino acid
explain: The statement is false because the initiation codon AUG does code for an amino acid (methionine / formylmethionine) while also setting the reading frame. The code being nonoverlapping, nearly universal, and degenerate are all true characteristics.
hint: Ask yourself what amino acid the start codon AUG actually inserts.

q: Which of the following amino acid substitutions could result from a SINGLE base-pair change in the codon (given Phe = UUU/UUC, Leu = UUA/UUG/CUN, Trp = UGG, Ile = AUU/AUC/AUA, Lys = AAA/AAG, Met = AUG)?
options: Phe -> Leu
Lys -> Phe
Trp -> Ile
Met -> Trp
explain: Phe (UUU/UUC) can become Leu (UUA/UUG) by changing only the third base, so Phe -> Leu needs a single substitution. Trp (UGG) -> Ile (AUU/AUC/AUA) and Lys (AAA/AAG) -> Phe (UUU/UUC) each differ at all three positions, and Met (AUG) -> Trp (UGG) differs at two positions, so none of those can arise from a single base change.
hint: Compare codons position by position; a single substitution changes exactly one of the three bases.

q: Suppose the genetic code were fully OVERLAPPING, so that each successive codon begins one nucleotide farther along the sequence. A stretch of nucleotides that a nonoverlapping triplet code would read as three codons (three amino acids) would, read this overlapping way, specify how many amino acids?
options: Nine
Three
Seven
Five
explain: Three nonoverlapping codons span 9 nucleotides. Reading a fully overlapping triplet code, a 3-base window starting at each successive position gives codons at start positions 1 through 7 (bases 1-3, 2-4, 3-5, 4-6, 5-7, 6-8, 7-9) = 7 codons, hence 7 amino acids from the same 9 bases, versus only 3 for a nonoverlapping code. This packing efficiency is exactly why researchers tested whether the real code overlaps (it does not).
hint: A nonoverlapping reading of three codons uses nine nucleotides; now slide a 3-base window one nucleotide at a time across those nine bases and count the triplets.

q: Examining a standard genetic code table (64 codons total), which conclusion can be drawn about the genetic code?
options: Three codons specify no amino acid, and most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon
Only the four codons beginning with A can serve as start signals
Each amino acid is specified by exactly one unique codon
All 64 codons specify an amino acid, leaving none for termination
explain: Of the 64 codons, three (UAA, UAG, UGA) are stop codons that specify no amino acid, and the code is degenerate, meaning many amino acids are read by multiple codons. A one-codon-per-amino-acid scheme would contradict this degeneracy.
hint: Count how many of the 64 codons are stops, and notice how many codons map to each amino acid.

q: The standard genetic code table (with codons such as AUG for methionine and UUU for phenylalanine) applies to which of the following organisms?
options: Only eukaryotes, since bacteria use an entirely different code
Only prokaryotes such as Escherichia coli
A lizard, a whale shark, an oak tree, Escherichia coli, and a mouse
Only vertebrate animals such as lizards, whale sharks, and mice
explain: The genetic code is nearly universal: bacteria (E. coli), plants (oak), and animals (lizard, whale shark, mouse) all use essentially the same codon assignments. Restricting it to only prokaryotes or only eukaryotes ignores this shared universality.
hint: Consider how broadly the same codon assignments are shared across the tree of life.

q: Using the standard genetic code, which of the following amino acid substitutions CANNOT be produced by a single base change in the codon?
options: Proline to alanine
Phenylalanine to isoleucine
Phenylalanine to arginine
Asparagine to lysine
explain: Phe (UUU/UUC) and Arg (CGU/CGC/CGA/CGG/AGA/AGG) share no codons that differ by a single base, so that swap needs at least two changes. In contrast, Phe->Ile (UUU->AUU), Pro->Ala (CCU->GCU), and Asn->Lys (AAU->AAA) each arise from a single base substitution.
hint: For each pair, write out codons and check whether they can differ at just one nucleotide position.

q: The alternating polymer 5'-TCTCTCTCTC-3' is transcribed and translated. If the code were a NON-overlapping doublet code, what kind of polypeptide would result?
options: Two homogeneous chains: one made entirely of one amino acid and the other entirely of a second amino acid
A chain containing many different amino acids in a rainbow-like pattern
A single chain that alternates between two amino acids
A chain of a single amino acid regardless of reading frame
explain: Read as non-overlapping doublets, the repeat gives only one word type per frame (UC-UC... or CU-CU...), so each of the two reading frames yields a chain of a single amino acid, producing two homogeneous chains of different amino acids. An alternating two-amino-acid chain is expected for overlapping doublet or triplet codes, not the non-overlapping doublet.
hint: Break the repeating sequence into non-overlapping two-letter words; how many distinct words appear, and does the frame matter?

q: How many distinct mRNA sequences could encode the peptide methionine-proline-tyrosine, counting the AUG start codon and a stop codon at the end? (Codon counts: Met=1, Pro=4, Tyr=2, stop=3)
explain: Methionine has 1 codon (AUG), proline 4, tyrosine 2, and there are 3 stop codons, so 1 x 4 x 2 x 3 = 24 distinct mRNAs. Adding rather than multiplying, or omitting the stop, gives the wrong count.
hint: Multiply the number of codon choices at each position, including the stop.

q: For the double-stranded DNA whose given strand reads 5'-CCATGCACCAGATCGCTTATTAAAT-3', how many complete open reading frames (start codon through in-frame stop codon) are present on this strand?
explain: Starting at the ATG (position 3), the frame reads ATG CAC CAG ATC GCT TAT TAA = Met-His-Gln-Ile-Ala-Tyr-STOP, one complete ORF. No other in-frame ATG-to-stop reading frame is present on this strand.
hint: Scan for ATG, then read codons in that frame until you hit an in-frame stop.

q: A DNA sequence can be read in six reading frames. For 5'-TAATGCCTAGTACCGGACTGAGTCAGTGTCTA-3', which single reading frame yields the largest open reading frame (the longest uninterrupted stretch coding a protein, MPSTGLSQCL)?
options: Reading frame three
Reading frame one
Reading frame six
Reading frame two
explain: Reading frame three translates without an internal stop into the 10-residue peptide MPSTGLSQCL, the longest ORF among the six frames. The other frames contain internal stop codons that interrupt translation, giving shorter products.
hint: The frame with the fewest internal stop codons and the longest peptide is the most likely coding frame.

q: A gene's coding (sense) strand reads 5'-TTA GAT CGC-3', so the transcribed mRNA has this same sequence with U replacing T. Reading the mRNA 5'->3', what is the correct set of encoded amino acids?
options: Leucine - glutamic acid - serine
Asparagine - leucine - alanine
Isoleucine - leucine - alanine
Leucine - aspartic acid - arginine
explain: The coding strand equals the mRNA sequence UUA GAU CGC (T->U), which reads Leu-Asp-Arg; the antiparallel tRNA anticodons are 3'-AAU-5', 3'-CUA-5', 3'-GCG-5'. Reading the complementary template strand instead, or mis-assigning codons, gives the incorrect amino acids.
hint: The coding strand matches the mRNA (U for T); read the codons directly, then look each up.

q: The nontemplate (coding) strand of a gene reads 5'-ATGATACTAAGGCCC-3'. Which sequence of amino acids does this mRNA encode?
options: His - Tyr - Stop - Arg - Pro
Met - Ile - Leu - Arg - Pro
Met - Tyr - Ser - Gly - Pro
Met - Ile - Thr - Lys - Ala
explain: The nontemplate strand matches the mRNA, so ATG ATA CTA AGG CCC reads as AUG AUA CUA AGG CCC = Met-Ile-Leu-Arg-Pro. Reading the complementary template strand instead would give a different, incorrect peptide.
hint: The nontemplate strand has the same sequence as the mRNA (with U for T); read it in codons from the 5' ATG.

q: How many different mRNA sequences can encode the peptide Met-Leu-Arg followed by a stop codon? (Codon counts: Met=1, Leu=6, Arg=6, stop=3)
explain: Methionine has 1 codon, leucine 6, arginine 6, and there are 3 stop codons: 1 x 6 x 6 x 3 = 108. Forgetting the stop codon (1 x 6 x 6 = 36) or the factor of 3 gives wrong totals.
hint: Multiply the codon choices at each position, and remember to include the stop codon.

q: In the genetic code, a 'sense codon' is a codon that:
options: pairs with the promoter to begin transcription
is never actually read during translation
specifies a particular amino acid to be added to the protein
signals the ribosome to stop translation
explain: A sense codon codes for an amino acid, in contrast to the three stop codons that terminate translation. Codons are read during translation, and promoters are DNA elements for transcription, not codon partners.
hint: Contrast it with a stop codon — what does a codon that 'makes sense' to the ribosome tell it to do?

q: To say the genetic code is 'degenerate' means that:
options: there are more codons than amino acids, so most amino acids are specified by more than one codon
some codons have no meaning and are never used
the same codon can specify different amino acids in different genes
each amino acid is specified by exactly one unique codon
explain: Degeneracy means the 61 sense codons outnumber the 20 amino acids, so several codons can encode the same amino acid. A one-to-one code would be non-degenerate, and a given codon still specifies only one amino acid (the code is unambiguous).
hint: Sixty-one sense codons must cover only 20 amino acids — what does that surplus imply?

q: Two different codons that specify the same amino acid are called:
options: nonsense codons
initiator codons
synonymous codons
overlapping codons
explain: Synonymous codons are distinct codons that encode the same amino acid, a consequence of the code's degeneracy. Nonsense codons are stop codons, overlapping codons share nucleotides (which the standard code does not), and the initiator codon is AUG.
hint: Think of the everyday word for two different words with the same meaning.

q: The statement that the genetic code is 'nonoverlapping' means that:
options: each codon can specify several different amino acids
the code contains gaps of unread nucleotides between codons
adjacent codons share one or two nucleotides to save sequence
each nucleotide is generally part of only one codon
explain: In a nonoverlapping code each nucleotide belongs to just one codon, so codons are read in consecutive, non-shared triplets. Sharing nucleotides between codons is the definition of an overlapping code, one codon still encodes a single amino acid, and the code has no unread gaps (it is commaless).
hint: If a base belonged to two codons at once, changing it would alter two amino acids — does the standard code work that way?

q: In molecular biology, the 'reading frame' of an mRNA refers to:
options: the loop of a tRNA that contains the anticodon
the region of DNA where RNA polymerase first binds
the single nucleotide that marks the poly(A) addition site
the way a nucleotide sequence is partitioned into consecutive triplet codons, set by the start codon
explain: The reading frame is how a sequence is divided into successive triplet codons, established by the start (initiation) codon and running to a stop codon. The polymerase-binding region is the promoter, the poly(A) site is set by AAUAAA, and the anticodon loop is a tRNA feature.
hint: It is about how the linear sequence gets grouped into threes — what fixes where that grouping starts?

q: The initiation codon of an mRNA is the first codon to specify an amino acid and is usually:
options: UAA
UGA
AUG
GGG
explain: The initiation codon is usually AUG, which codes for methionine (fMet in bacteria) and sets the reading frame. UAA and UGA are stop codons, and GGG is an ordinary glycine codon, not a start signal.
hint: The start codon sets the reading frame and codes for methionine — which triplet is that?

q: Which set of codons functions as the stop (termination) codons in the standard genetic code?
options: AUG, GUG, and UUG
UAC, AUG, and UGG
UUU, CCC, and AAA
UAA, UAG, and UGA
explain: UAA, UAG, and UGA are the three stop codons that signal termination and are recognized by release factors rather than tRNAs. AUG is the start codon, and the other listed triplets (e.g., UUU, CCC, UGG) code for amino acids.
hint: Recall the three mnemonic 'stop' codons — none of them codes for an amino acid.

q: What does it mean to say the genetic code is 'universal'?
options: Every organism uses exactly the same number of codons to encode a given protein.
The code can be read in any of the three reading frames without changing the protein.
Particular codons specify the same amino acids in almost all organisms.
A single amino acid is specified by only one codon in every organism.
explain: The near-universality of the code means a given codon (e.g., AUG = Met) specifies the same amino acid in almost all organisms, which is why genes can be expressed across species. It is degenerate (many amino acids have multiple codons), so 'only one codon per amino acid' is false, and only one reading frame is correct per message.
hint: Universal refers to how consistent codon-to-amino-acid assignments are across life, not to codon counts or reading frames.

q: You are working out a hypothetical genetic code for an organism whose mRNA is built from FIVE different bases instead of the usual four. Before doing any counting, what can you say about the codon length (number of bases per codon) at this first step?
options: It is 5 bases, matching the number of different bases
It is still fixed at 3 bases, because codon length never changes
It is an unknown number of bases that must be determined
It is 1 base, since five bases already exceed the 20 amino acids
explain: At the first step the codon length is an unknown you must determine: with five bases available, the minimum number of bases per codon needed to encode the required amino acids has to be calculated rather than assumed to be three. The familiar triplet code applies to the four-base natural system and cannot simply be carried over.
hint: With a different number of available bases, you cannot assume the codon is three letters long—you have to solve for how many bases are needed to specify enough amino acids.

q: A hypothetical genetic system uses an alphabet of 5 different bases, and each codon is exactly 1 base long. How many distinct codons can this system specify?
explain: With n=5 bases and codon length l=1, the number of codons is n^l = 5^1 = 5. Squaring (25) would apply only to length-2 codons.
hint: The number of possible words equals the alphabet size raised to the word length. Here the length is 1.

q: A hypothetical genetic system uses an alphabet of 5 different bases, and each codon is exactly 2 bases long. How many distinct codons can this system specify?
explain: With n=5 bases and codon length l=2, the number of codons is n^l = 5^2 = 25. Multiplying 5x2=10 treats the positions as additive rather than independent, which is incorrect.
hint: Each of the two positions can independently be any of the bases, so multiply the possibilities per position.

q: In a genetic coding system with n distinct bases and a fixed codon length of l bases, which expression gives the total number of distinct codons possible?
options: l^n
n + l
n^l
n x l
explain: Each of the l positions can independently be any of the n bases, so the total is n multiplied by itself l times, i.e. n^l. l^n reverses the roles of base count and codon length.
hint: Each of the l positions can be filled independently by any of the n bases. Think about how independent choices combine.

q: A hypothetical genetic system has 5 different bases available. What is the minimum codon length required to specify at least 4 distinct codons?
explain: With 5 bases, a codon length of 1 already yields 5^1 = 5 codons, which is at least 4, so length 1 suffices. A larger length is more than needed.
hint: Ask which codon length l makes 5^l first reach or exceed 4. Even a single base already gives you several options here.

q: A hypothetical genetic system has 5 different bases available. Using the rule that codon length l gives 5^l codons, what is the minimum codon length needed to specify 4, 20, and 30 codons, respectively?
explain: 5^1=5 covers 4 codons (length 1); 5^2=25 covers 20 codons (length 2); 30 exceeds 25, so length 3 (125) is needed. Choosing length 2 for 30 codons fails because 25 < 30.
hint: Compare each target to the powers of 5: 5^1=5, 5^2=25, 5^3=125. Find the smallest exponent that reaches or exceeds each target.

q: During bacterial translation, what facilitates cleavage (hydrolysis) of the completed polypeptide from the tRNA in the P site so the chain can be released?
options: Peptidyl transferase forming a new peptide bond
Elongation factor G (EF-G)
Release factors 1 and 2
Initiation factor 2 (IF2)
explain: Release factors (RF1/RF2 in bacteria) recognize stop codons in the A site and trigger hydrolysis of the bond between the polypeptide and the P-site tRNA. EF-G drives translocation and IF2 acts in initiation, neither releases the chain; peptidyl transferase forms peptide bonds rather than cleaving the finished chain.
hint: This happens at termination, when a stop codon enters the A site and no tRNA can read it.

q: A bacterial ribosome has assembled with fMet-tRNA^fMet occupying the P site and an empty A site over the next codon. This complex is now ready to begin which stage of translation?
options: Ribosome recycling
Initiation
Elongation
Termination
explain: Once the initiator tRNA sits in the P site with an open A site, initiation is complete and elongation begins as the next aminoacyl-tRNA enters the A site. Termination and recycling occur only after the polypeptide is finished.
hint: Initiation just finished (the initiator tRNA is already placed); what comes next is adding more amino acids.

q: Which sequence correctly orders these events of translation from first to last?
options: Small ribosomal subunit binds mRNA -> codon pairs with a complementary tRNA anticodon -> ribosome adds amino acids to the growing chain -> spent tRNAs exit the ribosome
Codon pairs with a tRNA anticodon -> small ribosomal subunit binds mRNA -> spent tRNAs exit -> amino acids are added
Ribosome adds amino acids -> small subunit binds mRNA -> codon pairs with anticodon -> spent tRNAs exit
Spent tRNAs exit the ribosome -> small subunit binds mRNA -> codon pairs with anticodon -> amino acids are added
explain: Initiation begins when the small ribosomal subunit binds mRNA; then each codon pairs with a complementary tRNA anticodon, the ribosome forms peptide bonds to extend the chain, and deacylated tRNAs finally exit through the E site. Any order where pairing or peptide addition precedes subunit binding is impossible.
hint: Translation must initiate (subunit binding) before codon-anticodon pairing, peptide-bond formation, and tRNA release can occur.

q: An mRNA is translated and the resulting peptide begins Met-Thr-Gln-Arg-Leu-His-Gly. Reading from the N-terminus, which amino acid is added third (immediately after threonine)?
options: Leucine
Arginine
Methionine
Glutamine
explain: The peptide is synthesized N-to-C as methionine, then threonine, then glutamine, so glutamine is the third residue. Arginine is fourth and leucine fifth, so those come later.
hint: Count residues from the starting methionine at the N-terminus.

q: Four snapshots of translation show: (A) the small ribosomal subunit and initiator tRNA assembling on the mRNA start codon, (C) the complete ribosome with a short peptide of two residues, (B) a longer elongating chain mid-mRNA, and (D) the finished polypeptide being released at a stop codon. What is the correct order of these stages?
options: D -> B -> C -> A
A -> B -> C -> D
C -> A -> B -> D
A -> C -> B -> D
explain: Translation proceeds from initiation (A, subunit assembling at the start codon), to early elongation with a short peptide (C), to later elongation with a longer chain (B), to termination and release (D). Ordering release or the longer chain before initiation reverses the process.
hint: Order by how much of the protein exists: initiation, then a short chain, then a longer chain, then release.

q: A synthetic mRNA is translated in a bacterial cell-free system to make Met-Pro-Ile-Ser-Ala. If EF-Tu is omitted from the system, what is the most likely outcome?
options: An abnormally long polypeptide forms because the ribosome cannot terminate at the stop codon
The 5' cap cannot be added, so the ribosome cannot recognize the message
Elongation fails and only a truncated (short) polypeptide is made because aminoacyl-tRNAs cannot be delivered to the A site
No polypeptide forms because the initiation complex never assembles
explain: EF-Tu delivers aminoacyl-tRNAs into the A site during elongation, so without it elongation stalls and only a short (or no) polypeptide is produced. Failure to assemble the initiation complex is caused by loss of IF-2/IF-3, failure to terminate is caused by loss of release factors, and prokaryotic mRNA has no cap.
hint: EF-Tu escorts a charged tRNA to a specific ribosomal site during the repeating cycle of adding residues — which step, and which site?

q: Which sequence correctly describes one round of the elongation phase of translation?
options: the ribosome moves down the mRNA -> a peptide bond forms -> a charged tRNA enters the A site
a charged tRNA enters the P site -> the ribosome moves down the mRNA -> a peptide bond forms
a peptide bond forms -> a charged tRNA enters the A site -> the ribosome moves down the mRNA
a charged tRNA enters the A site -> a peptide bond forms -> the ribosome moves down the mRNA
explain: In each elongation cycle a charged tRNA enters the A site (codon recognition), peptidyl transferase forms the peptide bond, then translocation moves the ribosome one codon down the mRNA. Charged tRNAs enter at the A site, not the P site, and bonding cannot precede delivery of the incoming amino acid.
hint: A new residue must arrive before it can be joined, and the bond must form before the ribosome can advance to read the next codon.

q: During elongation, which ribosomal site is where incoming charged (aminoacyl) tRNAs first enter?
options: The aminoacyl (A) site
The exit (E) site
The Shine-Dalgarno site
The peptidyl (P) site
explain: The A (aminoacyl) site accepts incoming charged tRNAs delivered by EF-Tu. The P site holds the tRNA bearing the growing polypeptide, and the E site is where deacylated tRNAs leave.
hint: The site's name matches the kind of tRNA it receives.

q: Which ribosomal site holds the tRNA carrying the growing polypeptide chain?
options: The acceptor stem
The exit (E) site
The peptidyl (P) site
The aminoacyl (A) site
explain: The P (peptidyl) site holds the tRNA attached to the growing polypeptide. The A site receives incoming charged tRNAs, the E site releases spent tRNAs, and the acceptor stem is a part of the tRNA itself, not a ribosomal site.
hint: The name of the site refers to the peptide it holds.

q: What happens at the exit (E) site of the ribosome?
options: The stop codon is read by a release factor.
Charged tRNAs enter and pair with the mRNA codon.
Deacylated (uncharged) tRNAs exit the ribosome.
The tRNA bearing the growing polypeptide is held in place.
explain: The E (exit) site is where deacylated tRNAs, having transferred their amino acid, leave the ribosome. Charged-tRNA entry is at the A site, the peptidyl-tRNA sits in the P site, and stop-codon recognition occurs at the A site via release factors.
hint: 'Exit' names what the tRNA does there, and the tRNA leaving has already given up its amino acid.

q: What is the role of elongation factor EF-Tu in bacterial translation?
options: It regenerates spent elongation factor by exchanging GDP for GTP.
It brings charged tRNAs to the ribosomal A site using GTP.
It uses GTP to translocate the ribosome to the next codon.
It hydrolyzes the bond releasing the finished polypeptide.
explain: EF-Tu-GTP delivers each aminoacyl-tRNA to the A site and hydrolyzes GTP upon correct codon pairing. EF-Ts regenerates EF-Tu, EF-G drives translocation, and release factors handle polypeptide release.
hint: Distinguish the factor that carries tRNAs in from the one that recharges it and the one that moves the ribosome.

q: What is the function of elongation factor EF-Ts?
options: It regenerates EF-Tu (recycling it for another round of tRNA delivery).
It promotes translocation of the ribosome along the mRNA.
It delivers charged tRNAs to the A site.
It stabilizes the 70S complex at the start codon.
explain: EF-Ts regenerates active EF-Tu by catalyzing the exchange of GDP for GTP so EF-Tu can deliver another charged tRNA. Delivering tRNAs is EF-Tu's job and translocation is EF-G's.
hint: Think of EF-Ts as the recycler that resets its partner factor after each delivery.

q: In translation, what does the term 'translocation' refer to?
options: The movement of the ribosome along the mRNA molecule.
The attachment of an amino acid to its tRNA by a synthetase.
The transfer of an amino acid from tRNA to the growing peptide chain.
The export of the finished protein across a membrane.
explain: Translocation is the movement of the ribosome along the mRNA (by one codon), shifting tRNAs from A to P to E sites. Peptide-bond transfer, tRNA charging, and protein export are distinct processes.
hint: The word describes the ribosome physically shifting relative to the message.

q: Which elongation factor uses GTP to move the ribosome to the next codon during translation?
options: EF-G
EF-Ts
IF-2
EF-Tu
explain: EF-G (translocase) hydrolyzes GTP to drive translocation, advancing the ribosome by one codon. EF-Tu delivers charged tRNAs, EF-Ts regenerates EF-Tu, and IF-2 acts in initiation.
hint: One elongation factor is dedicated to the translocation step itself, not to delivering or recycling tRNAs.

q: What is the role of the bacterial release factors RF-1, RF-2, and RF-3?
options: They assemble the small and large subunits at the start codon.
They bind at stop codons and trigger release of the completed polypeptide.
They deliver charged tRNAs to stop codons so translation can continue.
They add the poly(A) tail to the 3' end of the mRNA.
explain: Release factors recognize stop codons (RF-1 reads UAA/UAG, RF-2 reads UAA/UGA, RF-3 is a GTPase that stimulates release) and trigger hydrolysis that frees the finished polypeptide. Stop codons are not read by tRNAs, and these factors have no role in initiation or mRNA processing.
hint: Their name tells you the step: what has to happen when the ribosome reaches a stop codon?

q: If the Shine-Dalgarno sequence were deleted from a bacterial mRNA, what would be the most direct consequence for that transcript?
options: Translation would not occur because the ribosome could not properly initiate at the start codon
Transcription of the gene would fail to terminate
The mRNA would be translated but with a shifted reading frame
The mRNA would be spliced incorrectly
explain: The Shine-Dalgarno sequence base-pairs with the 16S rRNA of the small ribosomal subunit to position the ribosome over the start codon; without it, the ribosome cannot correctly initiate, so translation fails. It is a translational element, so it has no role in transcription termination or splicing.
hint: Recall what the Shine-Dalgarno sequence base-pairs with, and at which step of protein synthesis that pairing is required.

q: What is the Shine-Dalgarno sequence?
options: A eukaryotic promoter element that positions RNA polymerase II
The AAUAAA signal that directs cleavage and polyadenylation of the 3' end
A consensus sequence in the bacterial 5' UTR that serves as the ribosome-binding site
A tRNA recognition sequence read by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase during charging
explain: The Shine-Dalgarno sequence is a purine-rich consensus in the bacterial 5' UTR that base-pairs with 16S rRNA to recruit the small ribosomal subunit; it is a translation (not transcription) signal, unlike a promoter or the AAUAAA poly(A) signal.
hint: Think about how a prokaryotic small ribosomal subunit finds where to start on the message.

q: During initiation of translation in bacteria, which step requires energy (GTP hydrolysis)?
options: Dissociation of the 70S ribosome into free subunits before initiation
Base-pairing of the Shine-Dalgarno sequence with 16S rRNA
Binding of initiation factor 3 (IF3) to the 30S subunit
Binding of the charged fMet-tRNA^fMet to the initiation codon
explain: IF2-GTP delivers the charged fMet-tRNA^fMet to the start codon, and GTP hydrolysis accompanies this step and the joining of the large subunit. Shine-Dalgarno pairing, IF3 binding, and subunit dissociation are association/separation events that do not themselves consume GTP.
hint: Think about which factor escorts the initiator tRNA and carries a GTP that gets hydrolyzed as the large subunit joins.

q: During bacterial translation, which component is the LAST to join during formation of the 70S initiation complex?
options: The fMet-tRNA^fMet initiator
The large (50S) ribosomal subunit
The mRNA Shine-Dalgarno sequence
The small (30S) ribosomal subunit
explain: The 30S subunit assembles with mRNA and fMet-tRNA to form the 30S initiation complex, and the 50S large subunit joins last to complete the 70S initiation complex. The mRNA and initiator tRNA associate with the small subunit before the large subunit arrives.
hint: Initiation is built on the small subunit first; the complex only becomes '70S' when the second, larger piece arrives.

q: All of the following components are required for INITIATION of translation in a bacterial cell EXCEPT:
options: Initiation factors (IF1, IF2, IF3)
The 30S and 50S ribosomal subunits
fMet-tRNA^fMet
Release factor 1
explain: Release factor 1 functions in termination (recognizing stop codons), not initiation. Initiation factors, the initiator fMet-tRNA, and both ribosomal subunits are all needed to build the 70S initiation complex.
hint: One of these acts only at the very end of translation, not the beginning.

q: Regarding factor binding during bacterial translation initiation, which of the following pairings is correct?
options: Elongation factor Tu (EF-Tu) binds the small subunit to begin initiation
Initiation factor 3 (IF3) binds to the small (30S) ribosomal subunit
Initiation factor 3 (IF3) binds to the large (50S) ribosomal subunit
Release factor 1 binds to the small subunit to start initiation
explain: IF3 binds the 30S small subunit, helping prevent premature association with the 50S subunit and aiding mRNA positioning. Release factors act in termination and EF-Tu in elongation, not initiation.
hint: IF3's job includes keeping the subunits apart, so it must sit on the one that initiation is built around.

q: If an antibiotic disrupts the function of the small (30S) ribosomal subunit in bacteria, how does this prevent translation?
options: It blocks initiation, since the small subunit is where the initiation complex assembles on the mRNA
It prevents transcription of the mRNA in the first place
It blocks termination, since release factors act only on the small subunit
It blocks peptide-bond formation, which occurs in the small subunit
explain: The 30S subunit nucleates initiation by binding mRNA (via Shine-Dalgarno) and the initiator tRNA, so disrupting it blocks initiation. Peptide-bond formation is a function of the large subunit's rRNA, and transcription is a separate process that precedes translation.
hint: The small subunit is the platform that reads the Shine-Dalgarno sequence and positions the start codon.

q: Arrange these components of prokaryotic protein synthesis in the order they first act, from earliest to latest.
options: 30S initiation complex -> 70S initiation complex -> fMet-tRNA^fMet -> initiation factor 3 -> elongation factor G -> elongation factor Tu -> release factor 1
Initiation factor 3 -> fMet-tRNA^fMet -> elongation factor Tu -> elongation factor G -> 30S initiation complex -> 70S initiation complex -> release factor 1
fMet-tRNA^fMet -> initiation factor 3 -> 30S initiation complex -> 70S initiation complex -> elongation factor Tu -> elongation factor G -> release factor 1
fMet-tRNA^fMet -> 30S initiation complex -> initiation factor 3 -> elongation factor Tu -> 70S initiation complex -> elongation factor G -> release factor 1
explain: Initiation involves fMet-tRNA^fMet and IF3 assembling the 30S initiation complex, which matures into the 70S complex; elongation then uses EF-Tu (aminoacyl-tRNA delivery) and EF-G (translocation), and finally RF1 terminates. Placing elongation factors or the release factor before the initiation complexes violates the sequence of translation phases.
hint: Group by phase: initiation components (charged initiator tRNA and IF3, forming 30S then 70S complexes) come before elongation factors, which come before the release factor.

q: In bacterial translation initiation, what is the primary role of initiation factor IF-1?
options: It reads the Shine-Dalgarno sequence to position the start codon.
It enhances dissociation of the large and small ribosomal subunits.
It hydrolyzes GTP to drive translocation of the ribosome.
It uses GTP to deliver fMet-tRNA to the initiation complex.
explain: IF-1 promotes dissociation of the large and small subunits (and helps block the A site) during initiation. Delivering fMet-tRNA with GTP is IF-2's job, and translocation via GTP is EF-G's; the Shine-Dalgarno is recognized by the 16S rRNA, not IF-1.
hint: Two of the three initiation factors help keep the ribosomal subunits apart until the complex is ready; distinguish IF-1's job from IF-2's GTP-dependent delivery role.

q: Which bacterial initiation factor uses GTP and the charged initiator tRNA to deliver fMet-tRNA to the initiation complex?
options: IF-3
EF-Tu
IF-2
IF-1
explain: IF-2 is a GTPase that specifically escorts fMet-tRNA to the P site of the initiation complex. IF-1 and IF-3 keep the subunits apart, and EF-Tu delivers charged tRNAs during elongation (not initiation).
hint: One initiation factor is the GTP-dependent carrier of the special initiator tRNA; a different, similarly named elongation factor carries all the other charged tRNAs.

q: What is the function of initiation factor IF-3 in bacterial translation?
options: It binds the small ribosomal subunit and prevents the large subunit from binding prematurely.
It recognizes the stop codon to trigger release of the polypeptide.
It delivers fMet-tRNA to the start codon using GTP.
It catalyzes peptide-bond formation between the first two amino acids.
explain: IF-3 binds the 30S (small) subunit and prevents premature association of the 50S (large) subunit until initiation is complete. GTP-dependent fMet-tRNA delivery is IF-2's role, peptide bonds form later via peptidyl transferase, and stop-codon recognition is done by release factors.
hint: IF-3's job is to keep the small subunit free for assembling the initiation complex before the large subunit joins.

q: The bacterial 30S initiation complex consists of which set of components?
options: The small subunit plus mRNA, initiator fMet-tRNA, and initiation factors.
mRNA, EF-Tu, EF-G, and a deacylated tRNA in the E site.
The large subunit, mRNA, and all three release factors.
The small subunit and large subunit joined together with mRNA only.
explain: The 30S initiation complex is the small (30S) subunit assembled with mRNA, the initiator fMet-tRNA, and initiation factors; the large subunit joins afterward to form the 70S complex. Release and elongation factors are not part of this initiation step.
hint: The name '30S' tells you which subunit is present; the large subunit joins only at the next stage.

q: What defines the 70S initiation complex in bacteria?
options: The small subunit bound to mRNA and initiation factors, before the large subunit joins.
Two ribosomes translating the same mRNA simultaneously.
The complex of RNA polymerase and the leading ribosome during coupled transcription-translation.
The final complex containing both the small and large subunits, mRNA, and the initiator tRNA charged with fMet.
explain: The 70S initiation complex is the complete assembled ribosome (30S + 50S) with mRNA and fMet-tRNA positioned in the P site, ready for elongation. The small-subunit-only stage is the 30S complex, two ribosomes on one mRNA is a polyribosome, and the RNA-polymerase-plus-ribosome complex is the expressome.
hint: 70S is the assembled ribosome; contrast it with the earlier small-subunit-only stage.

q: Which RNA molecule catalyzes peptide bond formation during translation, acting as a ribozyme in the ribosome?
options: rRNA
miRNA
mRNA
tRNA
explain: The large-subunit rRNA is the peptidyl transferase ribozyme that forms peptide bonds. mRNA carries the coding sequence, tRNA hydrogen-bonds with codons and delivers amino acids, and miRNA regulates translation but does not catalyze peptide bonds.
hint: The peptidyl transferase activity of the large ribosomal subunit is RNA-based, not protein-based.

q: What is the large ribosomal subunit?
options: The subunit that holds the Shine-Dalgarno-pairing rRNA in bacteria
The complete ribosome formed once both subunits associate
The larger of the two subunits that make up a functional ribosome
The subunit that first binds mRNA and carries the decoding site
explain: The large ribosomal subunit is the bigger of the two subunits (housing the peptidyl transferase center); the mRNA-binding, decoding, and Shine-Dalgarno-pairing roles belong to the small subunit, and the assembled pair is the whole ribosome.
hint: A ribosome has two pieces of unequal size; this is simply the bigger one.

q: What is the small ribosomal subunit?
options: The smaller of the two subunits that make up a functional ribosome
A single tRNA-sized RNA that reads the codons
The subunit that houses the peptidyl transferase center
The intact ribosome after both subunits have joined on the mRNA
explain: The small ribosomal subunit is the lesser of the two subunits and binds mRNA to decode codons; peptidyl transferase activity resides in the large subunit, and the joined subunits form the complete ribosome.
hint: A ribosome has two pieces of unequal size; this is simply the smaller one.

q: Which statement correctly describes a polyribosome (polysome)?
options: A single mRNA molecule with several ribosomes translating it simultaneously
Several mRNAs joined end-to-end and read by one ribosome
A single ribosome that translates several different mRNAs at once
A cluster of tRNAs bound to a single codon
explain: A polyribosome is one mRNA being translated concurrently by multiple ribosomes, increasing protein output. The other options invert the relationship (one ribosome on many mRNAs), join messages that are actually independent, or confuse ribosomes with tRNAs.
hint: The prefix 'poly-' here refers to how many ribosomes are working on one message.

q: Which of the following is NOT an example of a posttranslational modification of a protein?
options: Attachment of a ribosome to the mRNA molecule
Cleavage of a signal peptide from the polypeptide
Addition of phosphate groups (phosphorylation)
Addition of carbohydrate chains (glycosylation)
explain: A ribosome binding mRNA is part of translation initiation, not a modification of a finished protein. Phosphorylation, proteolytic cleavage of a signal peptide, and glycosylation are all bona fide posttranslational modifications.
hint: Posttranslational means it happens to the protein AFTER it is made; one option describes an event that starts translation.

q: Many antibiotics inhibit bacterial protein synthesis. How can such an antibiotic kill or inhibit bacterial cells without harming the human host?
options: Human cells lack ribosomes, so only bacteria are affected
Bacterial and human ribosomes are identical, so timing of the dose provides selectivity
It binds the human ribosome to redirect it against bacteria
It binds selectively to bacterial (70S) ribosomes, which differ structurally from human (80S) ribosomes
explain: Bacterial 70S ribosomes differ enough from eukaryotic 80S ribosomes that many antibiotics bind the bacterial ribosome selectively, sparing the host. Human cells do have ribosomes, and the two ribosome types are not identical.
hint: Prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes differ in size and rRNA/protein composition, giving a drug something unique to grab.

q: Which posttranslational modification involves the addition of carbohydrate groups to a protein?
options: Glycosylation
Acetylation
Ubiquitination
Phosphorylation
explain: Glycosylation is the covalent attachment of carbohydrate (sugar) chains to a protein. Phosphorylation adds phosphate, acetylation adds acetyl groups, and ubiquitination attaches ubiquitin to tag proteins for degradation.
hint: The root 'glyco-' refers to sugars.

q: A functional protein is a heterohexamer composed of six subunits that are all different from one another. At minimum, how many genes are required to encode this protein?
options: Six
One
Two
Three
explain: Because all six subunits are unique polypeptides, each needs its own gene, so six genes are required. A single gene would suffice only for a homohexamer (six identical subunits).
hint: 'Hetero' means the subunits differ, so each distinct polypeptide needs its own coding sequence.

q: Which statement correctly characterizes the process of TRANSLATION (as distinct from replication and transcription)?
options: It is carried out by DNA polymerase, is semiconservative, and duplicates both DNA strands
It occurs on ribosomes, requires tRNAs, and adds amino acids to a growing polypeptide chain
It uses a single DNA strand as a template to produce an mRNA molecule
It occurs in the nucleus and produces two identical double-stranded DNA molecules
explain: Translation takes place on ribosomes, uses tRNAs to deliver amino acids, and builds a polypeptide. The DNA-polymerase/semiconservative description is replication, and using one DNA strand to make mRNA is transcription.
hint: Match the machinery to the product: which process actually builds a protein rather than a nucleic acid?

q: Every amino acid consists of a central alpha-carbon bonded to four groups. Which component of an amino acid is responsible for its unique chemical properties?
options: The carboxyl group
The R group (side chain)
The amino group
The alpha-carbon atom
explain: The alpha-carbon, amino group, and carboxyl group are common to all amino acids; only the R group (side chain) differs, giving each amino acid its distinctive size, charge, and polarity.
hint: Three of the four groups on the alpha-carbon are identical in every amino acid. Which one varies?

q: The secondary structure of a protein is best described as which of the following?
options: Local folding into regular patterns such as alpha-helices and beta-sheets, stabilized by hydrogen bonds
The linear N-to-C sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain
The overall three-dimensional shape of a single folded polypeptide
The assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into a functional complex
explain: Secondary structure refers to local hydrogen-bonded patterns like alpha-helices and beta-sheets. The linear sequence is primary structure, the full 3D fold of one chain is tertiary, and multi-subunit assembly (e.g., hemoglobin) is quaternary.
hint: Think about which level involves hydrogen-bonded local motifs rather than the full 3D fold or the amino acid order.

q: Which statement correctly describes a difference between translation in prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
options: In eukaryotes, the mRNA carries a 5' cap and poly(A) tail and pre-mRNA introns are removed before translation, whereas prokaryotes translate the transcript directly as its 5' end is released from the DNA
In prokaryotes, mRNA is capped and polyadenylated before ribosomes can bind, whereas eukaryotic mRNA is translated unmodified
In prokaryotes, translation occurs only on ribosomes attached to the endoplasmic reticulum, whereas eukaryotic translation occurs only on free ribosomes
In eukaryotes, translation is coupled to transcription and begins before the transcript detaches from DNA, whereas prokaryotes fully process mRNA first
explain: Eukaryotic mRNAs receive a 5' cap and poly(A) tail and have introns spliced out before export and translation, while prokaryotes lack a nucleus so translation of a message begins as soon as its 5' end emerges from the DNA. Coupled transcription-translation is a prokaryotic feature, not eukaryotic, and prokaryotes use free ribosomes (they have no ER), so the reversed options are wrong.
hint: Which cell type separates transcription and translation into two compartments, and which one can translate an mRNA while it is still being made?

q: Translation is best defined as the process of:
options: removing introns and joining exons in a pre-mRNA transcript
assembling amino acids into proteins using the information in mRNA
copying a DNA sequence into a complementary RNA molecule
synthesizing a new DNA strand from a DNA template
explain: Translation is the ribosome-mediated assembly of amino acids into a polypeptide directed by mRNA codons. Copying DNA into RNA is transcription, intron removal is splicing, and copying DNA into DNA is replication.
hint: Which of the central-dogma steps produces protein as its output rather than a nucleic acid?

q: The 'one gene, one enzyme' hypothesis proposed by Beadle and Tatum states that:
options: genes are made of protein and enzymes copy them into RNA
each enzyme is encoded by many genes acting together
a single gene directs the synthesis of every enzyme in the cell
each gene functions by encoding a separate enzyme
explain: Beadle and Tatum concluded that genes work by specifying enzymes, with each gene encoding one separate enzyme. It is not many genes per enzyme, not one gene for all enzymes, and genes are DNA rather than protein.
hint: The name of the hypothesis gives the ratio — how many genes per enzyme?

q: How does the 'one gene, one polypeptide' hypothesis modify the original 'one gene, one enzyme' idea?
options: It says a single gene encodes all of a cell's polypeptides
It states that only genes coding for structural (non-enzyme) proteins exist
It recognizes that each gene encodes a separate polypeptide chain, not only enzymes
It proposes that polypeptides, rather than genes, carry heritable information
explain: The refinement recognizes that not all proteins are enzymes and many are built from multiple chains, so each gene is said to encode one polypeptide. It does not claim one gene makes all polypeptides, does not exclude enzymes, and genes (DNA), not polypeptides, are the heritable information.
hint: Many proteins are made of more than one chain, and not every protein is an enzyme — how does that broaden the original claim?

q: Which set of chemical groups is attached to the central (alpha) carbon of a standard amino acid?
options: two carboxyl groups and two amino groups
a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base
an amino group, a hydrogen, a carboxyl group, and an R (side-chain) group
a hydroxyl group, a methyl group, and a carbonyl group only
explain: Each amino acid has a central carbon bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and a variable R side chain. Phosphate/sugar/base describe a nucleotide, and the other combinations do not match the amino-acid backbone.
hint: An amino acid is named for two of its groups; the fourth substituent is the variable part that distinguishes the 20 amino acids.

q: A peptide bond is the chemical bond that:
options: joins complementary bases across the two DNA strands
links a phosphate to the sugar of the next nucleotide in a nucleic acid backbone
connects adjacent amino acids in a protein
attaches an amino acid to its tRNA during charging
explain: A peptide bond covalently links successive amino acids in a polypeptide. Base pairing is hydrogen bonding, sugar-phosphate links are phosphodiester bonds, and the amino-acid-to-tRNA linkage is an ester bond made during charging.
hint: The word 'peptide' points you to which class of macromolecule this bond builds.

q: What is a polypeptide?
options: a chain of amino acids linked by peptide bonds
a ring of sugar and phosphate carrying a nitrogenous base
a strand of nucleotides joined by phosphodiester bonds
a single amino acid attached to a tRNA
explain: A polypeptide is a chain of amino acids joined by peptide bonds (also called a protein). A nucleotide strand is a nucleic acid, a single charged residue is an aminoacyl-tRNA, and the sugar-phosphate-base ring describes a nucleotide.
hint: Break the word apart: 'poly' plus the bond type named in the previous concept.

q: What is a polyribosome (polysome)?
options: A single mRNA molecule being translated by several ribosomes at once.
A cluster of tRNAs all charged with the same amino acid.
A ribosome that contains several copies of each rRNA.
A protein made of several identical polypeptide subunits.
explain: A polyribosome is one mRNA with multiple ribosomes translating it simultaneously, increasing protein output per transcript. It is not a single ribosome, a group of tRNAs, or a multi-subunit protein.
hint: 'Poly' + 'ribosome' points to how many ribosomes share one message.

q: In bacteria, what is the 'expressome'?
options: The complex formed by RNA polymerase and the leading (first) ribosome.
The complete set of proteins expressed by a cell at a given time.
A single mRNA with many ribosomes translating it at once.
The complex of a synthetase bound to its charged tRNA.
explain: The expressome is the physical complex of RNA polymerase coupled to the first trailing ribosome, a feature of coupled transcription-translation in bacteria. A single mRNA with many ribosomes is a polyribosome, the full protein set is the proteome, and a synthetase-tRNA pair is a charging complex.
hint: This structure physically links the machine making mRNA to the machine reading it, reflecting coupled transcription-translation.

q: What is a molecular chaperone?
options: An enzyme that attaches amino acids to tRNAs.
A factor that reads stop codons to release the polypeptide.
A ribozyme that catalyzes peptide-bond formation.
A molecule that assists the proper folding of another protein.
explain: A molecular chaperone assists the correct folding of another protein (preventing misfolding/aggregation) without becoming part of the final structure. Charging is done by synthetases, peptide bonds by peptidyl transferase, and termination by release factors.
hint: Think about what helps a newly made polypeptide reach its correct three-dimensional shape.

q: Which feature of gene regulation is characteristic of PROKARYOTES (e.g., E. coli) rather than eukaryotes?
options: Each mRNA transcript typically specifies only a single protein
Pre-mRNA splicing must occur to remove introns before translation
mRNA can be transcribed and translated simultaneously because there is no nuclear membrane
Transcription occurs in the nucleus while translation occurs in the cytoplasm
explain: Prokaryotes lack a nuclear envelope, so ribosomes can begin translating an mRNA while it is still being transcribed (coupled transcription-translation). Splicing, one-protein-per-transcript, and spatially separated transcription/translation are all hallmarks of eukaryotes, whose mRNA must be processed and exported before translation.
hint: Which of these can only happen in a cell that has no nucleus separating the DNA from the ribosomes?

q: In an operon, a regulatory protein acts as an activator that binds a control region and stimulates transcription by RNA polymerase. This type of transcriptional control is called:
options: Catabolite repression
Positive control
Negative control
Attenuation
explain: Positive control is defined by a regulatory protein (an activator) that stimulates/increases transcription. Negative control is the opposite: a repressor binds and shuts transcription off. Attenuation and catabolite repression are specific regulatory mechanisms, not the general term for activator-driven stimulation.
hint: Focus on whether the regulatory protein turns transcription ON or OFF when it binds.

q: Cancer cells frequently show inappropriate gene expression. Which category of gene is LEAST likely to be inappropriately expressed (turned on or off at the wrong time) in cancer cells?
options: A tumor-suppressor gene that restrains the cell cycle
A constitutive (housekeeping) gene expressed at a steady level in essentially all cells
A gene normally expressed only during embryonic development
A proto-oncogene that promotes cell division
explain: Constitutive (housekeeping) genes are expressed continuously at a stable level in most cells and are not under the tight on/off regulation whose disruption drives cancer, so they are least likely to be inappropriately expressed. Proto-oncogenes, tumor-suppressor genes, and developmentally restricted genes are exactly the regulated genes whose misexpression contributes to cancer.
hint: Which class of gene is by definition expressed at a roughly steady level regardless of conditions, so there is little regulatory switch to break?

q: Many marine bacteria produce light (bioluminescence) only when their population reaches a high density, a phenomenon called quorum sensing. How is this linked to gene regulation?
options: Light production requires the loss of the bacterial genome at high density
Light-production genes are permanently on and light output simply reflects total cell number
Light-production genes are transcribed only at high bacterial cell densities, when an accumulated signaling molecule triggers them
Each bacterium mutates its DNA to gain light genes as the population grows
explain: In quorum sensing, a diffusible autoinducer accumulates as the population grows, and only at high density does it reach the threshold that switches on transcription of the light-production (lux) genes. The genes are regulated (conditionally transcribed), not constitutively on, and no DNA mutation or genome loss is involved.
hint: The cells sense how crowded they are via a diffusible molecule that builds up with density. That signal switches specific genes on—so expression is conditional, not constant.

q: Operons can be classified by how their transcription responds to conditions. Which description best characterizes an INDUCIBLE operon?
options: Transcription is turned on when a specific molecule (inducer) is present
Sufficient amounts of the gene product inhibit further transcription
Transcription stops when the repressor gene product is activated
Transcription occurs constantly regardless of environmental conditions
explain: An inducible operon is switched ON when a specific inducer molecule appears. Product-mediated shutdown and repressor-activated shutdown both describe repressible operons, while constant expression regardless of conditions describes a constitutive operon.
hint: An inducer switches transcription ON. Contrast that with a repressible operon, where accumulating product switches it OFF.

q: In a negative inducible operon (such as lac), the regulatory protein controls transcription. Which type of transcriptional regulator provides the negative control in an inducible operon?
options: An active activator
An active repressor
An inactive activator
An inactive repressor
explain: Negative control in an inducible operon is exerted by an active repressor that binds the operator and blocks transcription until an inducer inactivates it. An inactive repressor characterizes a negative repressible operon; activators (active or inactive) provide positive control.
hint: Negative control means a repressor is doing the regulating. In an inducible system it is ON (bound, blocking) until an inducer inactivates it.

q: The blob operon encodes enzymes that convert compound A into compound B and is controlled by regulatory gene S. In wild-type cells the enzymes are made only in the ABSENCE of compound B. When gene S is mutated (non-functional), the enzymes are made whether or not compound B is present. What is the most likely role of the gene S product, and what type of operon is this?
options: Gene S encodes an activator that requires compound B as a coactivator; the operon is repressible
Gene S encodes an activator that requires compound A as a coactivator; the operon is inducible
Gene S encodes a repressor that requires compound A as a corepressor; the operon is inducible
Gene S encodes a repressor that uses compound B as a corepressor; the operon is repressible because wild-type transcription is repressed when compound B is present
explain: Because a non-functional gene S leads to constitutive expression, the wild-type S product normally represses the operon, i.e. it is a repressor. Since repression happens only when compound B is present, B acts as the corepressor that activates the repressor, making the operon repressible. An activator would give the opposite loss-of-function phenotype (loss of expression).
hint: Losing gene S makes the operon always ON, so its product normally turns things OFF (a repressor). It turns things off specifically when compound B is around, so B must activate that repressor.

q: In E. coli, structural genes A, D, and E (order O-A-D-E, where O is an operator) encode enzymes that catalyze valine biosynthesis. When cellular valine is LOW the enzymes are made, and when valine is high they are shut off. Studies of operator mutants show that when the operator is nonfunctional, expression of A, D, and E is high (constitutive). What type of regulatory protein binds this operator?
options: An inducer
An activator
A coactivator
A repressor
explain: Because a nonfunctional operator gives high, constitutive expression, the protein that normally binds there must have been blocking transcription; that is the definition of a repressor (here a valine-corepressor system). An activator would give the opposite result: losing its site would decrease, not increase, expression. An inducer is a small molecule, not the DNA-binding regulatory protein, and a coactivator assists activation rather than blocking it.
hint: Knocking out the operator turns expression ON constitutively. If losing the protein's binding site causes MORE expression, was the protein turning genes on or off?

q: The mmm operon contains sequences A, B, C, and D (some structural, some regulatory) and encodes enzymes 1 and 2. In the wild type, both enzymes are synthesized when the metabolite mmm is ABSENT and shut off when mmm is present. Based on this behavior, is the mmm operon inducible or repressible, and why?
options: Inducible, because both enzymes are made when mmm is present
Repressible, because a mutation in one sequence blocks production of both enzymes
Repressible, because both enzymes are made when mmm is absent
Inducible, because a mutation in one sequence produces both enzymes
explain: A repressible operon is transcribed by default and is switched OFF when the effector molecule (a corepressor, here mmm) accumulates, which matches enzymes being made only when mmm is absent. An inducible operon is the reverse: OFF by default and turned ON by the inducer, so making enzymes in the absence of the molecule rules out induction. The choice citing a mutation blocking both enzymes describes a promoter defect and does not establish whether the operon is repressible.
hint: The default state is ON, and adding the small molecule turns it OFF. Which operon class is normally transcribed until a signal molecule shuts it down?

q: In Streptococcus pneumoniae, the com regulon is activated when secreted competence-stimulating peptide (CSP) accumulates and binds a membrane receptor; the receptor then activates a regulator protein that stimulates transcription of com-regulon genes. Does this system use positive or negative control, and how?
options: Positive control: CSP activates the membrane receptor, which turns on transcription of the com-regulon genes
Positive control: CSP binds the receptor and stimulates a regulator that suppresses transcription at multiple loci
Negative control: CSP acts as a repressor of the com pathway whose removal stimulates transcription
Negative control: CSP activates the membrane receptor, which turns on transcription of com-regulon genes
explain: Because the activated regulator turns transcription on, this is positive control by definition. The negative-control options contradict the fact that CSP switches genes on, and calling it positive control while saying it 'suppresses transcription' is internally inconsistent.
hint: Ask whether the regulatory signal turns transcription ON or OFF. A regulator that binds and stimulates transcription defines one type of control.

q: A geneticist studies a bacterial inducible operon whose regulator gene makes a repressor. In this context, what best defines an 'inducible operon'?
options: An operon that is transcribed only during times of extreme stress
An operon that is not regulated and is always transcribed
A unit of bacterial DNA controlled by a single promoter that encodes related genes
An operon that is transcribed only when a specific molecule is present
explain: An inducible operon is normally off and is transcribed only when a specific inducer molecule is present. 'Always transcribed with no regulation' defines a constitutive operon, restriction to extreme stress is not the definition, and 'a unit of DNA under one promoter encoding related genes' defines an operon in general.
hint: An inducible operon is normally OFF. Something specific must appear to switch it on. Contrast that with a constitutive operon that is always on.

q: In a bacterial operon under negative control, which statement best defines a repressor?
options: A stretch of bacterial DNA that physically halts operon transcription
A regulatory protein that greatly reduces transcription only when it is NOT bound to the operator
A protein that supercoils bacterial DNA so tightly that transcription cannot occur
A regulatory protein that greatly decreases operon transcription by binding to the operator
explain: A repressor is a trans-acting regulatory protein that lowers transcription by binding the operator. It is not DNA (that describes the operator itself), and it represses when bound to (not when off) the operator; the reversed version and the supercoiling protein are distractors.
hint: A repressor is a protein (encoded by a separate regulatory gene), and it turns transcription DOWN by physically occupying the operator so RNA polymerase can't proceed.

q: In an inducible operon, where is the repressor located in the ABSENCE of the inducer?
options: Bound to the promoter alongside RNA polymerase
Diffusing freely in the cytoplasm, unable to contact any DNA site
Bound to the operator, blocking transcription of the structural genes
Bound to the inducer and released from the operator
explain: In an inducible operon the default (no-inducer) state is OFF: the active repressor binds the operator and blocks transcription. Inducer-bound-and-released describes the ON state, and the repressor binds the operator, not the promoter.
hint: With no inducer around, nothing changes the repressor's shape, so its DNA-binding site is active and it sits where it shuts the operon down.

q: In an inducible operon, where is the repressor located in the PRESENCE of the inducer?
options: Bound to the inducer while still occupying the operator simultaneously
Bound to the inducer and no longer bound to the operator, allowing transcription
Bound to the operator even more tightly, fully blocking transcription
Degraded by proteases so that no repressor protein remains in the cell
explain: In an inducible operon the inducer binds the repressor, causing a conformational change that releases it from the operator so transcription can occur. The repressor is inactivated (not degraded) and cannot stay on the operator once inducer-bound.
hint: The inducer is an allosteric effector: binding it changes the repressor's shape so its operator-binding site no longer fits.

q: In DNA methylation as a mechanism of gene regulation, which nucleotide base is methylated?
options: Adenine
Thymine
Guanine
Cytosine
explain: In eukaryotic gene regulation, cytosine is methylated (to 5-methylcytosine), typically at CpG dinucleotides. Adenine methylation occurs in some prokaryotic systems but is not the base referenced for epigenetic silencing here, and guanine and thymine are not the methylated bases in this context.
hint: Think about CpG islands; which of the two bases in that dinucleotide gets the methyl group in eukaryotes?

q: What are CpG islands?
options: Segments of protein rich in the amino acids cysteine and glycine
DNA regions containing many cytosine bases positioned adjacent to guanine bases
Regions of mRNA rich in cytosine and guanine that form the poly(A) tail
Clusters of ribosomes translating the same mRNA simultaneously
explain: CpG islands are DNA regions with a high density of cytosine bases directly adjacent (5' to) guanine bases, and their methylation state controls gene expression. They are not ribosome clusters (polysomes), mRNA/poly(A) features, or protein motifs.
hint: The 'p' stands for the phosphodiester bond between the two bases in the name; think DNA and methylation.

q: Heavily methylated DNA in a gene's regulatory region is usually associated with:
options: Repression of transcription
Increased rate of translation
Activation of transcription
Removal of introns from the transcript
explain: Heavy DNA methylation, typically at promoter CpG islands, is generally associated with transcriptional repression (silencing). It does not activate transcription, act at the level of translation, or carry out intron splicing.
hint: Recall whether adding methyl groups to promoter cytosines tends to switch genes on or off.

q: Demethylation of DNA allows _____ to add acetyl groups to histones, promoting a more open, transcriptionally active chromatin state.
options: acetyltransferases
deacetylases
methyltransferases
DNA polymerases
explain: Acetyltransferases (histone acetyltransferases) add acetyl groups to histones, loosening chromatin and favoring transcription. Methyltransferases add methyl (not acetyl) groups, deacetylases remove acetyl groups, and DNA polymerases synthesize DNA rather than modify histones.
hint: The enzyme's name should match the group being added (acetyl), not removed.

q: Why are monozygotic (identical) twins particularly useful in epigenetic studies?
options: They have twice the normal number of chromosomes, amplifying epigenetic effects.
Although they are genetically identical, they sometimes exhibit differences in how they look and behave.
They always develop the exact same diseases at the same ages, proving genes control everything.
They have completely different DNA sequences, so any similarity must be epigenetic.
explain: Because monozygotic twins share an identical genome, any phenotypic differences in appearance or behavior between them point to epigenetic (non-sequence) influences. Identical twins do not have different DNA sequences, do not always share identical disease outcomes, and have a normal chromosome number.
hint: If the DNA sequence is the same for both twins, what could explain any phenotypic differences between them?

q: In rats, maternal grooming and licking of pups produces lasting, beneficial changes in the offspring's stress response through epigenetic modification. If the grooming and licking were instead performed by an unrelated adult rat rather than the biological mother, would the same benefits to the offspring be expected?
options: No, because the effect requires DNA inherited from the biological mother
No, because epigenetic marks can only be established during gamete formation
Yes, but only if the unrelated adult is genetically identical to the pup
Yes, because epigenetic changes are driven by the environmental stimulus, so the identity/relatedness of the caregiver does not matter
explain: The offspring benefit arises from an environmental stimulus (the grooming/licking behavior) that triggers epigenetic changes such as altered DNA methylation of stress-response genes, so an unrelated adult providing the same care would produce the same effect. The effect does not depend on genetic relatedness or on marks being set only during gamete formation.
hint: The benefit comes from a behavior (an environmental input), not from shared genes. Does an environmentally triggered epigenetic change care who provides the behavior?

q: In the broad framing used for gene regulation, how do chromatin modifications such as DNA methylation and histone changes regulate gene expression?
options: By editing the DNA base sequence of the promoter itself
By altering the amino acid sequence encoded within the gene
By changing the chromatin's accessibility, they determine whether genes are available for transcription
By changing the anticodon of the tRNAs that read the gene's codons
explain: Chromatin modifications work by altering how accessible the DNA is: open (accessible) chromatin allows transcription while condensed chromatin blocks it, so modified regions become available (or unavailable) for transcription. These marks do not change the underlying DNA base sequence, the encoded amino acids, or tRNA anticodons.
hint: Epigenetic marks do not change the DNA sequence—they change how tightly the DNA is packaged. What does looser vs tighter packaging control?

q: The malaria parasite Plasmodium switches which var gene is expressed (antigenic variation) using epigenetic marks rather than DNA sequence changes. Which chromatin modification most plausibly marks the actively transcribed var gene?
options: Removal of all histones from the var locus
The H3K4me3 (trimethylation of histone H3 at lysine 4) activating mark
Dense CpG methylation across the var promoter
The H3K9me3 heterochromatin-associated silencing mark
explain: H3K4me3 is a well-established activating histone mark enriched at the promoters of transcriptionally active genes, so it plausibly marks the single expressed var gene. H3K9me3 and dense CpG methylation are silencing marks associated with the repressed var copies, and stripping all histones from a locus is not the mechanism of var activation.
hint: You want a histone mark associated with active, open chromatin at a transcribed gene—one classically found at promoters of genes that are turned ON.

q: Paramutation at the maize (corn) b1 locus is a heritable change in gene expression in which one allele epigenetically alters another, without any change in DNA sequence. Which factor is LEAST likely to be involved in paramutation at b1?
options: Heritable chromatin state changes
Nucleotide sequence differences among the b1 alleles
Differences in DNA methylation between the alleles
Small RNAs that direct silencing
explain: Paramutation is epigenetic, mediated by DNA methylation, small RNAs, and heritable chromatin states rather than by any change in the DNA base sequence, so nucleotide differences among the alleles are least likely to be the cause. The interacting b1 alleles can be identical in sequence yet still differ in expression through these epigenetic mechanisms.
hint: Paramutation is an epigenetic phenomenon—it works without altering the DNA code. Which listed factor is a change in the DNA sequence itself?

q: A patient produces no protein and no mRNA from gene X. Sequencing shows gene X carries an abnormal expansion of cytosine/guanine-rich (CpG) repeats near its transcription start site, but the coding sequence is intact. Which hypothesis best explains the lack of expression?
options: The expanded repeats introduce a premature stop codon that truncates the protein
The expansion deletes the poly(A) signal so no stable mRNA can form
The repeats change the reading frame of the coding sequence
The chromatin of gene X is more heavily deacetylated (and its CpG island more methylated) than in healthy individuals, silencing it
explain: Because there is no mRNA, transcription is being shut off—CpG-rich repeat expansions near a start site recruit DNA methylation and histone deacetylation, producing condensed, silenced chromatin. A premature stop codon or a frameshift would still allow mRNA to be made, and deleting the poly(A) signal would still permit transcription of an unstable transcript, so none of those explain the complete absence of mRNA.
hint: No mRNA at all means transcription itself is blocked, not that the protein is mistranslated. CpG-rich repeats near a start site invite what kind of silencing chromatin state?

q: Eukaryotes regulate genes at several levels: chromatin structure, the transcription apparatus, RNA processing, RNA interference, and translation initiation. The observation "Acetylation of histones facilitates transcription" is an example of regulation at which level?
options: Activity of the transcription apparatus
RNA interference
Changes in chromatin structure
RNA processing
explain: Histone acetylation neutralizes positive charges on histone tails, loosening the DNA-histone interaction and opening chromatin, so it acts at the level of chromatin structure. Competition of repressors with activators would be the transcription apparatus, alternative splicing is RNA processing, and siRNA targeting is RNA interference.
hint: Acetyl groups on histone tails loosen how tightly DNA is wound around the nucleosome. What is that packaging called?

q: Chromatin modifications alter transcription by changing DNA accessibility. Attachment of acetyl groups to the N-terminal tails of histones by histone acetyltransferases has what effect on transcriptional activity?
options: Activates transcription by loosening chromatin
Both activates and inactivates depending on which residue is modified
Has no effect on transcriptional activity
Inactivates transcription by condensing chromatin
explain: Histone acetylation neutralizes lysine positive charges, loosens histone-DNA contacts, and opens chromatin, thereby activating transcription. DNA (CpG) methylation is the repressive mark, and the context-dependent 'both' outcome describes histone methylation, not acetylation.
hint: Acetylation removes positive charge from histone tails, weakening their grip on the negatively charged DNA backbone. Does that open or close the chromatin?

q: H3K4me3 (trimethylation of histone H3 lysine 4) is an activating mark typically found near the transcription start sites of actively transcribed genes. If a 300-kilobase stretch of DNA contains four H3K4me3-marked regions, how many genes does it most likely contain?
options: Four or more genes
Zero genes
Exactly four genes
At most four genes
explain: Four H3K4me3 marks indicate at least four active promoters, so there are four or more genes; additional genes could be present but transcriptionally silent and therefore unmarked. 'Exactly four' or 'at most four' wrongly assume every gene must carry the mark, and 'zero' contradicts the four active start sites.
hint: Each mark flags at least one active start site, but the mark can't tell you about genes that are silent (unmarked). Is four a floor or a ceiling on the gene count?

q: Histone proteins package eukaryotic DNA into nucleosomes and chromatin. In terms of gene expression, histone proteins generally:
options: Stabilize condensed chromosomes specifically during transcription
Regulate gene expression by reducing the rate of transcription
Strengthen the hydrogen bonds between complementary DNA bases
Increase the transcription rate of the genes they package
explain: By compacting DNA into nucleosomes, histones limit access of the transcription machinery and thereby reduce transcription unless the chromatin is remodeled or modified. Histones bind the DNA backbone (electrostatically), not the base pairs, so they do not strengthen base-pairing hydrogen bonds, and condensed chromatin is generally incompatible with active transcription.
