hint: Wrapping DNA tightly around a histone core makes it harder, not easier, for polymerase to reach the DNA. What does that do to transcription by default?

q: A temperature-sensitive mutation inactivates a histone acetyltransferase in cultured human cells: above 38 degrees C the enzyme is nonfunctional. What is the most likely outcome if these cells are grown at 40 degrees C?
options: Cells grow because histone acetylation still occurs and opens chromatin for transcription
Cells fail to grow because histones become deacetylated, opening chromatin and over-stimulating transcription
Cells grow because histone deacetylation restores chromatin structure and represses transcription
Cells fail to grow because histones are not acetylated, so chromatin stays closed and transcription cannot occur
explain: At 40 degrees the acetyltransferase is nonfunctional, so histones cannot be acetylated; chromatin remains condensed and closed, transcription of needed genes fails, and the cells do not grow. Options claiming acetylation still occurs contradict the dead enzyme, and an open/over-stimulated chromatin outcome is the opposite of losing an acetyl-ADDING enzyme.
hint: At 40 degrees the enzyme that ADDS acetyl groups is dead. Without acetylation, is chromatin more open or more closed, and can genes needed for growth be transcribed?

q: Researchers want to infer the physical appearance of an extinct hominid, such as a Denisovan, from ancient DNA recovered from bone. Since no living tissue or RNA survives, which gene-expression mechanism can be reconstructed from the DNA itself to predict which genes were active?
options: Methylation patterns of the DNA can be used to infer the expression of known genes
Enhancers and promoters can be sequenced and quantified to measure gene expression
RNA segments can be sequenced and quantified to identify the amounts of specific proteins
Transcription factors can be quantified to determine which genes were transcribed
explain: DNA methylation is a stable epigenetic mark carried on the DNA itself, so it can be read from ancient bone DNA to infer which known genes were expressed. Quantifying transcription factors, RNA, or protein is impossible because those molecules do not survive; merely counting enhancers/promoters in the sequence does not reveal expression level.
hint: Proteins and RNA degrade over millennia, but a chemical mark left directly on the DNA base can survive and reflects whether a gene was silenced or active.

q: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (BWS) causes larger-than-average size and overgrowth-related medical problems. A six-month-old boy is 72 cm long and weighs 9.8 kg with no other apparent symptoms. Would a clinician conclude he has BWS?
options: Yes, because he is above the 95th percentile for length and weight
No, because there were no other apparent BWS symptoms
No, because his atypical length and weight could be caused by something else
Additional information is needed to properly diagnose the patient
explain: Body size alone is not diagnostic of BWS, so more information (e.g., genetic testing, other clinical features) is needed before concluding the child has or does not have the syndrome. A flat 'yes' based only on size, or a flat 'no' based on absent extra symptoms, over-commits without adequate data.
hint: Large size alone is nonspecific. Think about what a responsible diagnosis requires before ruling BWS in or out.

q: A child with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (an overgrowth disorder driven by imprinted genes on chromosome 11) has both parents' genomes sequenced and compared to the child's. Which imprinting-related genotype is expected in this child?
options: Paternal uniparental disomy
Maternal uniparental disomy
Maternal uniparental monosomy
Paternal uniparental monosomy
explain: BWS overgrowth is associated with paternal uniparental disomy, where both chromosome-11 copies come from the father, over-representing growth-promoting paternally expressed genes. Maternal disomy would reduce growth, and monosomy (a single copy) is not the mechanism described.
hint: BWS is an overgrowth syndrome, and paternally expressed genes tend to promote growth. Disomy means two copies from one parent; monosomy would mean only one copy total.

q: About 20% of Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (an overgrowth disorder) cases arise from uniparental disomy of chromosome 11, which carries genes affecting fetal growth. Using the genetic conflict (parental conflict) hypothesis, would these children carry two paternal or two maternal copies of chromosome 11, and why?
options: Paternal, because selective pressure on paternal alleles favors smaller offspring
Maternal, because selective pressure on maternal alleles favors larger offspring
Maternal, because selective pressure on maternal alleles favors smaller offspring
Paternal, because selective pressure on paternal alleles favors larger offspring
explain: The genetic conflict hypothesis holds that paternal alleles are selected to maximize offspring growth (drawing maternal resources), while maternal alleles restrain it; two paternal copies therefore produce the BWS overgrowth phenotype. Options pairing paternal with 'smaller' or maternal with 'larger' invert the hypothesis.
hint: Under the conflict hypothesis, the father's alleles 'want' to maximize this offspring's growth while the mother's alleles conserve her resources across offspring. BWS is overgrowth, so which parent's double dose fits?

q: Paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 11 (which carries several imprinted growth genes) accounts for only about 20% of Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome cases. Which other change to chromosome 11 can also cause BWS?
options: Partial trisomy of the whole chromosome
Loss of an entire copy of chromosome 11
An atypical methylation pattern at the imprinted region
Partial monosomy of the whole chromosome
explain: Besides paternal uniparental disomy, BWS can result from atypical methylation of the imprinted region (as well as single-gene mutations and localized duplications, translocations, or deletions). Note: after feedback, gain or loss of a whole chromosome-11 copy (partial trisomy/monosomy) is not part of these cases, so those options are wrong.
hint: BWS is fundamentally an imprinting/dosage problem at specific loci. Think about changes that alter the expression or dosage of those imprinted genes rather than gain or loss of a whole chromosome copy.

q: Which statement describes a feature shared by both prokaryotic and eukaryotic genes?
options: Pre-mRNA introns are removed by a spliceosome before translation.
Genes are organized into operons transcribed as a single polycistronic mRNA.
DNA-binding proteins influence the ability of RNA polymerase to initiate transcription of a gene.
Transcription and translation are physically separated by a nuclear envelope.
explain: In both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, DNA-binding regulatory proteins modulate whether RNA polymerase can initiate transcription. Operons/polycistronic mRNA are characteristically prokaryotic, while spliceosomal intron removal and nuclear compartmentalization are eukaryotic features.
hint: Which of these regulatory principles is universal, versus features unique to one domain?

q: Why are transcriptional regulator proteins necessary for gene expression in eukaryotes?
options: The basal transcription apparatus can only produce minimal levels of transcription without them.
They supply the ribonucleotides used as substrates for RNA synthesis.
They are required to remove introns from the pre-mRNA transcript.
RNA polymerase cannot synthesize RNA in the 5'->3' direction without them.
explain: The basal transcription apparatus alone yields only minimal (basal) transcription; regulator proteins are needed to raise expression to functional levels. RNA polymerase intrinsically synthesizes 5'->3' without them, intron removal is done by the spliceosome, and nucleotide substrates are not provided by regulatory proteins.
hint: The general (basal) machinery alone gives only a baseline; what do activators add on top of that?

q: How do response elements regulate transcription?
options: They are the codons within mRNA that are read by the ribosome.
They serve as binding sites for transcriptional activators that respond to specific stimuli.
They are the amino acid sequences that target a protein for degradation.
They are enzymes that chemically modify RNA polymerase.
explain: Response elements are DNA sequences that act as binding sites for transcriptional activators (e.g., hormone-receptor complexes) triggered by particular stimuli. They are DNA regulatory sites, not mRNA codons, enzymes, or protein degradation signals.
hint: A response element is a stretch of DNA; ask what binds it when a signal (like a hormone) appears.

q: What best describes the mechanism of action of chromatin-remodeling complexes?
options: They bind directly to DNA and reposition nucleosomes to make the DNA more accessible
They add methyl groups to cytosine bases in CpG islands to silence promoters
They cleave double-stranded RNA into small interfering RNAs that guide silencing
They synthesize the poly(A) tail that stabilizes mature mRNA
explain: Chromatin-remodeling complexes use ATP to bind DNA and slide or reposition nucleosomes, exposing DNA so transcription factors and polymerase can access it. Cytosine methylation is done by DNA methyltransferases, RNA cleavage into siRNAs is done by Dicer, and poly(A) tailing is an mRNA-processing step—none of these are the remodeling mechanism.
hint: The name says it: these complexes physically reorganize the packaging of DNA around histones rather than chemically tagging bases.

q: What is one mechanism by which chromatin-remodeling complexes reposition nucleosomes to regulate transcription?
options: They covalently join adjacent nucleosomes into a permanent fiber
They excise nucleosomal DNA and religate it in a new order
They translate histone mRNAs directly on the chromatin fiber
They change the conformation of DNA and/or nucleosomes so that DNA bound to nucleosomes assumes a more exposed conformation
explain: Chromatin-remodeling complexes alter the conformation of DNA and/or nucleosomes so that the wrapped DNA becomes more exposed and accessible to the transcription machinery. They do not cut and religate DNA, permanently fuse nucleosomes, or translate mRNA.
hint: Remodelers physically loosen or shift the DNA-histone arrangement so the DNA becomes reachable—no cutting of DNA and no changing the base sequence.

q: How can mRNA processing influence the rate of translation of a transcript?
options: It has no effect on translation, which depends only on ribosome number
Processing modifications (such as the 5' cap and poly(A) tail) can alter how efficiently the mRNA is translated
It changes the anticodons of the tRNAs used during translation
Processing determines the amino acid sequence independently of the codons
explain: mRNA processing modifications—notably the 5' cap and poly(A) tail—are recognized by initiation factors and influence how efficiently and how often a transcript is translated. Translation rate is not fixed by ribosome number alone, and processing does not set the amino acid sequence apart from the codons or alter tRNA anticodons.
hint: Features added during processing (think of the two ends of a mature eukaryotic mRNA) are recognized by the translation machinery. Could altering them change translation efficiency?

q: Some modifications increase the instability of an mRNA, shortening its lifetime in the cell. How does increasing mRNA instability affect expression of the gene?
options: It increases expression because unstable mRNAs are translated faster
It increases expression by stabilizing the encoded protein
It has no effect because expression is set only at transcription
It decreases expression because the mRNA is degraded sooner, so less protein is made
explain: A more unstable mRNA is degraded sooner, so it is available for translation over a shorter time and yields less protein, decreasing gene expression. mRNA stability is a genuine post-transcriptional control point, so expression is not determined by transcription alone.
hint: A shorter-lived mRNA spends less time available to ribosomes. What does that do to the total amount of protein produced?

q: In gene regulation, what is an INSULATOR?
options: A regulatory protein that blocks transcription by binding the operator
A short DNA sequence recognized by a repressor protein
A DNA element that blocks the signal between an enhancer and a promoter
A DNA sequence upstream of a gene that signals the start of transcription
explain: An insulator is a DNA element that, when placed between an enhancer and a promoter, blocks the enhancer from activating that promoter (a boundary/blocking element). The upstream start signal describes a promoter, the operator-binding transcription blocker is a repressor, and the short repressor-recognition sequence is the operator.
hint: Think about what physically separates a gene from the influence of a nearby enhancer, keeping that enhancer from acting on the wrong promoter.

q: How do eukaryotic cells use alternative RNA splicing to regulate gene expression?
options: It determines which genes become underexpressed relative to others
It determines which genes are transcribed into mRNA in the first place
It determines how quickly certain proteins are degraded after translation
It determines which proteins are produced from a single gene by joining different combinations of exons
explain: Alternative splicing joins different subsets of exons from the same pre-mRNA, so one gene can yield multiple distinct protein isoforms. It acts post-transcriptionally, so it does not decide which genes are transcribed, nor does it control protein degradation rates.
hint: Splicing happens AFTER transcription, on the pre-mRNA. If you keep or drop different exons, what changes about the final product?

q: In yeast, the non-coding gene SRG1 lies just upstream of the serine-biosynthesis gene SER3. RNA polymerase binds the SRG1 promoter and transcribes through the adjacent SER3 promoter, repressing SER3; mutations in the SRG1 promoter relieve this repression. Which mechanism best explains how SRG1 transcription represses SER3?
options: The SRG1 protein product binds the SER3 promoter and prevents its transcription
SRG1 RNA recruits enzymes that modify histones to form heterochromatin over SER3
Transcription machinery moving across the SER3 promoter physically blocks transcription factors from binding it (transcriptional interference)
SRG1 transcription is so rapid that it titrates general transcription factors away from SER3
explain: The act of transcribing SRG1 sends polymerase across the SER3 promoter, occluding it so SER3 transcription factors cannot bind (transcriptional interference); this is why disrupting the SRG1 promoter, and thus its transcription, relieves repression. SRG1 is explicitly non-protein-coding, so a protein product is impossible, and the effect depends on transcription over the promoter rather than heterochromatin formation or titration.
hint: The polymerase literally runs through the SER3 promoter. What happens to anything trying to bind that promoter while a polymerase is passing over it?

q: In the yeast GAL system, the activator Gal4p is required to transcribe GAL genes such as GAL1, Gal80p is a negative regulator that binds and masks the Gal4p activation domain, and Gal3p is needed to disrupt the Gal80p-Gal4p interaction so transcription can proceed. Which alteration would INHIBIT GAL1 transcription by keeping the Gal4p activation domain masked from other proteins?
options: A GAL80 deletion that removes Gal80p entirely
A GAL3 mutation that makes Gal3p bind Gal4p more tightly
A GAL4 mutation that deletes the negative-regulator gene
A GAL80 mutation that prevents Gal3p from disrupting the Gal80p-Gal4p complex
explain: If a GAL80 mutation makes the Gal80p-Gal4p complex resistant to disruption by Gal3p, Gal80p permanently masks the Gal4p activation domain and GAL1 stays off. Deleting GAL80 (or its protein) would REMOVE the inhibitor and allow transcription, the opposite effect; GAL4 does not encode the negative regulator; and making Gal3p grip Gal4p tightly does not keep Gal80p in place.
hint: You want Gal80p to STAY stuck on Gal4p. Which change keeps that inhibitory complex locked together rather than pulling Gal80p off?

q: Consider the arrangement: Enhancer I - Enhancer II - (promoter of gene B) - gene B, with gene A elsewhere. Enhancer I activates gene A but is blocked from gene B by an insulator, and Enhancer II activates gene B. What happens if the insulator is moved to sit BETWEEN Enhancer II and the promoter of gene B?
options: The repositioned insulator shuts off gene A expression
The insulator causes Enhancer II to hyperactivate gene B
The insulator now lets Enhancer I activate gene B transcription
The insulator now blocks Enhancer II from stimulating gene B transcription
explain: An insulator blocks enhancer-promoter communication only when interposed between them, so placing it between Enhancer II and the gene B promoter cuts off Enhancer II's activation of gene B. Insulators block rather than enhance, so they cannot cause hyperactivation, and this move does not create a new path for Enhancer I or affect gene A.
hint: An insulator only blocks an enhancer when it sits physically BETWEEN that enhancer and the promoter it would act on. Which enhancer is now separated from gene B's promoter?

q: The wild mustard Arabidopsis thaliana is a premier model organism for plant genetics. Which property makes it especially useful as a model organism?
options: It has a large, highly polyploid genome that is easy to sequence
It self-fertilizes and cross-fertilizes and needs little space or care, with a short generation time
It requires large greenhouse space and long generation times for reliable crosses
It produces only a few, very large seeds per generation
explain: Arabidopsis is prized because it grows quickly with a short generation time, needs little lab space or care, and can both self- and cross-fertilize, enabling large-scale genetic crosses. It actually has a small (not large polyploid) genome and produces many small seeds, so the descriptions of a large polyploid genome, few large seeds, or heavy space/time demands are false.
hint: Good genetic model organisms tend to be small, fast, cheap, and flexible in how they can be mated. Which choice describes that profile?

q: U937D cells make abundant creatine kinase (CK-B) mRNA and load ribosomes onto its 5' end, yet the protein is not translated. Introducing many short RNA fragments that contain 3' UTR consensus sequences switches on CK-B protein synthesis without changing CK-B mRNA levels; fragments lacking those 3' UTR sequences do nothing. Which explanation best fits these results?
options: Degradation of the added 3' UTR RNAs supplies free nucleotides that let stalled ribosomes resume
Translational repressor proteins that normally bind the CK-B 3' UTR are titrated away onto the introduced 3' UTR RNA, freeing ribosomes to translate CK-B
The short 3' UTR RNAs recombine with CK-B mRNA to make a longer, more translatable transcript
The added RNA raises total RNA levels, increasing ribosome and factor numbers and overall efficiency
explain: A repressor protein bound to the CK-B 3' UTR blocks translation; flooding the cell with decoy RNA carrying the same 3' UTR sequence sequesters that repressor, releasing CK-B mRNA for translation, which explains why only 3' UTR-containing RNA works and why mRNA amount is unchanged. Recombination into a longer transcript, nucleotide supply, and generic RNA-concentration effects would not require the specific 3' UTR consensus and are not supported.
hint: Only RNA carrying the 3' UTR consensus works, and mRNA levels never change. What must those specific sequences be soaking up that was previously silencing the message?

q: Eukaryotic mammalian cells regulate gene expression at many levels. Which of the following is NOT a mechanism used to regulate gene expression?
options: Splicing and degrading RNA
Replicating strands of DNA
Expressing transcription factors
Changing chromatin structure
explain: DNA replication copies the genome for cell division and is not a means of regulating how genes are expressed. Chromatin remodeling, RNA splicing/degradation, and transcription-factor expression are all bona fide levels of eukaryotic gene-expression control.
hint: Three of these change how much of a gene's product is made; one is simply copying the genome to pass it on. Which one isn't about tuning expression?

q: In Drosophila melanogaster sex determination, the Sxl protein controls a splicing cascade. In XX (female) embryos, Sxl protein is produced, which leads to functional Tra protein; Tra then directs female-specific splicing of Dsx pre-mRNA, and the resulting Dsx protein drives female development. What is the corresponding sequence of events in an XY (male) embryo?
options: Sxl protein is produced, alternative splicing yields functional Tra protein, and Dsx protein drives male development
Sxl protein is not produced, Tra protein directs male-specific splicing of Dsx pre-mRNA, and Dsx drives male development
Sxl protein is not produced, alternative splicing yields non-functional Tra protein, and Dsx protein drives male development
Sxl protein is produced, but non-functional Tra protein directs female-specific splicing of Dsx pre-mRNA
explain: In XY embryos Sxl is not produced, so tra is spliced into a non-functional Tra protein; with no functional Tra, Dsx pre-mRNA defaults to the male splice and Dsx drives male development. Male-specific splicing of Dsx is the default (Tra-independent) outcome, so Tra never 'directs male-specific splicing'; that statement is a distractor.
hint: Males are the default pathway here: without the initial regulator, the downstream splicing factor is never made in a working form, so nothing directs the female-specific splice.

q: Researchers ran RNA-seq on transforming versus non-transforming Streptococcus pneumoniae and displayed the results as a heat map where low expression is red, medium is black, and high is blue. Six candidate com-regulon genes (A-F) are shown. Genes A, B, D, and F show similar color (similar expression) between the two samples, whereas genes C and E change sharply between samples. Which genes are most likely required for transformation?
options: Genes C and E
Genes A and B
Genes A, B, D, and F
Gene C only
explain: Genes required for transformation should be differentially expressed between transforming and non-transforming cells; only C and E show large color/expression differences between the samples. Note: after feedback the color key is low = red, medium = black, high = blue, and the genes to pick are those with the biggest between-sample expression difference. Genes with similar color in both conditions (A, B, D, F) are not specifically linked to transformation.
hint: A gene needed specifically for transformation should be turned up (or down) when the cell switches states, not held at the same level in both conditions.

q: Researchers compared RNA-seq heat maps of three Streptococcus pneumoniae strains: non-transforming, transforming, and a CSP-deficient (CSP-) strain. The CSP- heat map looks largely like the non-transforming strain and unlike the transforming strain. Is CSP a regulator of transformation, and why?
options: No, because the CSP- expression pattern is significantly different from the transforming strain
Yes, because the CSP- expression pattern is largely similar to that of the non-transforming strain
No, because the CSP- expression pattern is largely similar to that of the non-transforming strain
Yes, because the CSP- expression pattern is significantly different from the transforming strain
explain: Removing CSP makes the expression profile resemble the non-transforming (off) state, meaning CSP is required to switch on the transformation program, so it is a regulator. The 'No' options misinterpret that similarity, and pointing only to the difference from the transforming strain without linking it to loss of CSP does not support the correct 'Yes' reasoning.
hint: If removing a factor makes the cell's expression revert to the 'off' (non-transforming) state, that factor was needed to turn transformation on.

q: In Streptococcus pneumoniae, CSP is exported and binds a membrane CSP receptor, which activates a regulator protein that switches on the com regulon (CSP -> receptor -> regulator -> transcription). Three heat maps show the expression outcome when each of the three genes is separately deleted, arranged left to right. Bright blue = high expression, bright red = low expression. Which deletion does the left-most map represent if the maps go: near-total loss of com-regulon expression, then progressively less-complete loss?
options: Regulator deletion
CSP deletion
CSP receptor deletion
A double deletion of CSP and its receptor
explain: The intended left-to-right order is CSP receptor deletion, CSP deletion, regulator deletion; the receptor deletion most completely abolishes com-regulon expression because the signal cannot be received at all, whereas secreted CSP can partly be supplied by neighboring cells. A combined CSP+receptor deletion is not one of the three single-gene knockouts tested, so it is a distractor.
hint: CSP is secreted and can be shared between cells, so losing it in one cell is the least severe hit; knocking out a component that cannot be complemented from outside is more severe. Rank by how completely each loss shuts the pathway.

q: In Drosophila, the tra transcript has four exons (A, B, C, D) and is alternatively spliced by sex. Female tra mRNA omits exon B (yielding functional Tra protein), whereas male tra mRNA retains all four exons (yielding non-functional Tra). Based on exon-probe expression, which exons are present in the spliced mRNA of each sex?
options: Male: A, B, C present but D absent; Female: all four present
Male: A, B, C, D present; Female: A, C, D present but B absent
Female: B and C absent; Male: all four present
Female: A, B, C, D present; Male: A, C, D present but B absent
explain: Males use the default splice retaining all four exons (A, B, C, D), which includes a premature stop that makes Tra non-functional; females splice out exon B, so their mRNA contains only A, C, and D. Swapping the sexes or removing exon D instead of B misrepresents the pathway.
hint: The functional (female) protein comes from removing an exon that contains a premature stop; the male default keeps every exon. Figure out which sex is missing exon B.

q: In eukaryotic gene regulation, how is an 'insulator' best defined?
options: A DNA sequence that stimulates maximal transcription of distant genes on the same DNA molecule
A complex of proteins that repositions nucleosomes
A DNA sequence that blocks an enhancer from acting on a promoter in a position-dependent manner
An enhancer-like sequence that represses transcription
explain: An insulator blocks enhancer action in a position-dependent way, shielding a promoter when located between it and an enhancer. The other choices define an enhancer, a silencer, and a chromatin-remodeling complex, respectively.
hint: This element acts as a barrier: it only blocks an enhancer when placed between the enhancer and the gene it would otherwise activate.

q: Which set of properties correctly describes a transcriptional ENHANCER in eukaryotes?
options: Binds the operator to limit transcription of downstream genes
Blocks an enhancer's effect on a neighboring gene
Regulates only a single adjacent gene and always decreases its transcription
Can regulate many genes, act over great distances, and increase transcription
explain: Enhancers are cis-regulatory elements that increase transcription, can act over long distances, and can influence multiple genes. Limiting an enhancer's reach describes an insulator, operator binding describes a prokaryotic repressor, and single-gene repression is not enhancer behavior.
hint: Think about what makes enhancers special versus promoters: they boost transcription, can act far away (even thousands of bp), and are not limited to one gene. Three of these options describe other elements entirely.

q: On a DNA fragment, genes are arranged in order A, B, C, then an insulator, then D, with a single enhancer positioned to act on A, B, and C. Which gene is shielded from that enhancer by the insulator?
options: C
A
B
D
explain: The insulator sits between C and D, so it blocks the enhancer from acting on D, the gene beyond it. Genes A, B, and C lie on the enhancer's side of the insulator and are still enhanced.
hint: An insulator blocks an enhancer's effect from reaching whatever lies on the far side of it. Which gene is on the opposite side of the insulator from the enhancer?

q: On a DNA fragment ordered A, B, C, [insulator], D with one enhancer acting on the A-B-C side, which genes show INCREASED transcription due to the enhancer?
options: Only D
A, B, and C
Only A
A, B, C, and D
explain: Genes A, B, and C are on the enhancer's side of the insulator, so all three have increased transcription; D is insulated and unaffected. Because enhancers can act over distance and on multiple genes, it is not limited to just A.
hint: The insulator sits between C and D. Everything on the enhancer's side of that boundary gets boosted; the gene past the insulator does not.

q: On a DNA fragment ordered A, B, C, D, an enhancer boosts every gene between itself and the nearest insulator. If an insulator is placed between B and C, which genes are shielded from an enhancer located on the A-B side?
options: A and B
Only D
A, B, C, and D
C and D
explain: With the enhancer on the A-B side and an insulator between B and C, genes A and B are enhanced (T) while C and D lie beyond the insulator and are shielded (X). An insulator only blocks the enhancer for genes on its far side.
hint: The insulator draws a line: genes on the enhancer's side get transcription-boosted (T), genes on the far side are blocked (X). With the enhancer near A-B and the insulator between B and C, which genes fall on the far side?

q: What is the function of the lac operator?
options: It encodes the repressor protein that regulates the operon.
It is the site where RNA polymerase binds to begin transcription.
It is the DNA sequence bound by the lac repressor protein.
It is the site where CAP-cAMP binds to activate transcription.
explain: The operator is the DNA sequence to which the Lac repressor binds; occupancy blocks transcription. The site where RNA polymerase binds is the promoter, and the CAP-cAMP binding site is the CAP site, not the operator. The repressor is encoded by lacI, not by the operator sequence.
hint: The operator is a cis-acting DNA site; ask which protein physically sits on it to block transcription.

q: A regulatory system in which glucose is used preferentially and the metabolism of other sugars is repressed while glucose is present is called:
options: Catabolite repression
Attenuation
Induction
Feedback inhibition
explain: Catabolite repression is the preferential use of glucose that represses catabolism of alternative sugars (low glucose raises cAMP, which activates CAP). Attenuation regulates amino-acid operons via a leader region, feedback inhibition acts on enzyme activity rather than sugar-source choice, and induction turns an operon on rather than repressing it.
hint: Think about why E. coli won't bother making enzymes for lactose when glucose is available; it involves cAMP levels.

q: What is the function of lacI in the regulation of the lac operon?
options: It encodes a repressor that, in the absence of lactose, binds the operator and blocks expression of the structural genes.
It encodes the enzyme beta-galactosidase that cleaves lactose.
It encodes an activator that binds the promoter to stimulate transcription when lactose is present.
It is the operator sequence to which the repressor binds.
explain: lacI encodes the Lac repressor, which binds the operator in the absence of lactose and blocks transcription of lacZYA. It is not an activator or the operator DNA itself, and beta-galactosidase is encoded by lacZ (a structural gene), not lacI.
hint: lacI is a regulatory gene, not a structural one; recall what its product does when no inducer is around.

q: In the lac operon, when lactose is absent the operon is OFF. Which molecule directly enables transcription of the structural genes when lactose becomes available?
options: Lactose itself, which binds the promoter to recruit RNA polymerase
Allolactose, the inducer that binds and inactivates the Lac repressor
The Lac repressor, which binds the operator to switch the operon on
beta-galactosidase, which binds the operator to displace the repressor
explain: When lactose is present, a small amount is converted by beta-galactosidase to allolactose, which binds the Lac repressor and inactivates it so it releases the operator, allowing transcription of lacZYA. The repressor represses (it does not activate), lactose is not the direct inducer, and beta-galactosidase converts lactose to allolactose rather than binding the operator.
hint: Lactose is first converted to a slightly different sugar that is the true signal. That signal molecule pulls the repressor off the operator.

q: The lac operon is under negative regulation: the Lac repressor (product of the lacI/I gene) is normally active and binds the operator to block transcription, and allolactose (an isomer of lactose) is the inducer that inactivates the repressor. Which event INCREASES the concentration of lac gene products?
options: The operator sequence mutates to bind the Lac repressor more tightly
Allolactose is absent from the cell
The Lac repressor remains bound to the operator
Allolactose binds the Lac repressor, causing it to dissociate from the operator
explain: When allolactose binds the Lac repressor it changes shape and releases the operator, freeing RNA polymerase to transcribe lacZYA, so gene products increase. A repressor that stays bound, or the absence of allolactose, keeps the operon OFF and decreases products.
hint: Gene products rise when the repressor comes OFF the DNA. Which listed event pulls the repressor away from the operator?

q: Beta-galactosidase production from the lac operon depends on both lactose (the inducer) and glucose (which lowers cAMP and reduces CAP activation). Given conditions - (1) lactose present, glucose absent; (2) lactose absent, glucose present; (3) both present; (4) both absent - under which condition is beta-galactosidase production GREATEST?
options: Condition 1: lactose present, glucose absent
Condition 2: lactose absent, glucose present
Condition 3: both lactose and glucose present
Condition 4: both lactose and glucose absent
explain: Maximal beta-galactosidase requires lactose (to inactivate the repressor) and low glucose (so cAMP is high and CAP activates the promoter), which is exactly condition 1. Condition 3 has lactose but glucose lowers cAMP-CAP, and conditions without lactose leave the repressor bound.
hint: Maximal transcription needs two things at once: the inducer present AND cAMP-CAP active. Glucose kills the second, so you want lactose yes, glucose no.

q: A mutation renders the lac promoter nonfunctional so that RNA polymerase can no longer bind it. What is the consequence for the lac operon?
options: Expression of the structural genes only when glucose is also present
Constitutive (continuous) expression of the lac structural genes regardless of lactose
Lack of expression of the lac structural genes even in the presence of lactose
Overproduction of the Lac repressor protein
explain: Without a functional promoter, RNA polymerase cannot bind and initiate, so the structural genes are not transcribed even when lactose (inducer) is present. Constitutive expression results from operator or repressor defects, not a dead promoter, and the promoter mutation does not increase repressor production.
hint: If RNA polymerase cannot dock at the promoter, can transcription start at all, even when the inducer is present?

q: In the lac operon, a mutation is described as trans-acting if the affected element produces a diffusible product that can regulate genes on either DNA molecule in a partial diploid. Mutations in which lac component are trans in effect?
options: The lacI gene
The lacO operator
The lacZ structural gene
The lacP promoter
explain: lacI encodes the diffusible Lac repressor protein, which can travel through the cytoplasm and bind operators on either DNA molecule in a partial diploid, making it trans-acting. The operator (lacO) and promoter (lacP) are DNA sequences that only affect the operon physically attached to them, and a lacZ mutation affects only its own encoded enzyme, so those are cis in effect.
hint: Which element encodes a diffusible protein product (rather than being a DNA binding site), so it can act on an operon located on a separate DNA molecule?

q: In a haploid E. coli cell, which lac operon genotype produces beta-galactosidase both in the presence AND in the absence of lactose? (lacI^s = super-repressor that cannot bind inducer; lacO^c = constitutive operator that cannot bind repressor; lacP+ = functional promoter; lacZ+ = functional structural gene.)
options: lacI+ lacP+ lacO+ lacZ+ lacY+
lacI^s lacP+ lacO+ lacZ+ lacY+
lacI+ lacP- lacO^c lacZ+ lacY+
lacI^s lacP+ lacO^c lacZ+ lacY+
explain: The lacO^c constitutive operator cannot bind repressor, so with a functional promoter and lacZ+ the operon transcribes beta-galactosidase regardless of lactose (the lacI^s super-repressor is simply overridden because there is no operator for it to bind). A lacI^s super-repressor with a normal operator (lacO+) would stay OFF even with lactose, and a lacP- promoter mutation would prevent transcription entirely, so those genotypes fail the both-conditions requirement.
hint: For enzyme to be made even without lactose, the repressor must be unable to shut the operon off. Which mutation frees the operator from repressor control while keeping a working promoter and lacZ?

q: In the lac operon, a mutation is cis-acting if it only affects genes located on the same DNA molecule as the mutation. Mutations in which lac component are cis in effect?
options: The gene encoding RNA polymerase
The lacI gene
The gene encoding the CAP (catabolite activator) protein
The lacO operator
explain: The lacO operator is a DNA sequence that serves as a binding site; it can only affect transcription of the operon physically linked to it, making its mutations cis-acting. lacI, the CAP gene, and the RNA polymerase gene all encode diffusible proteins that act in trans on operons anywhere in the cell.
hint: Which element is a DNA binding site (not a gene making a diffusible protein), so it can only influence the operon physically attached to it?

q: Assume all lac promoters are wild type and lactose is present (no glucose). For the partial diploid genotype I^S O+ L+ / (chromosome only, no F'), where I^S is a super-repressor that cannot bind the inducer and O+ is a wild-type operator, what is the expression pattern of the lacZYA (L) genes?
options: Inducible expression (off without lactose, on with lactose)
No transcription (the operon cannot be induced)
Constitutive expression only when glucose is also present
Constitutive expression (always on)
explain: The I^S super-repressor cannot bind allolactose, so it stays locked on the wild-type operator even when lactose is present, giving no transcription. It is not inducible (lactose has no effect) and not constitutive, since the operator is still functional and remains blocked.
hint: A super-repressor (I^S) ignores the inducer and stays clamped on the operator. If it can never let go, can lactose ever turn the genes on?

q: In a lac operon mutant analysis, mut1 cells show HIGH lac permease with or without lactose (constitutive). In a partial diploid where wild-type cells carry F' lac from mut1 (wt / F' lac from mut1), permease is normally inducible (off without lactose). But mut1 cells carrying F' lac from wild type (mut1 / F' lac from wt) remain constitutive. Which defect in mut1 best explains this trans-complementation pattern?
options: Deletion of the operator
Defective promoter
Deletion of the repressor (lacI) gene
Defective CAP-binding site
explain: A deleted lacI gene makes no repressor, giving constitutive expression; because the repressor is a diffusible (trans-acting) protein, a wild-type lacI on an F' plasmid restores inducibility, and a wild-type chromosome rescues the mutant plasmid. An operator deletion is cis-acting and cannot be complemented in trans, so it would stay constitutive regardless of a wild-type copy.
hint: The mutant is constitutive, and a wild-type copy on a plasmid can rescue it (trans-complementation). Only a diffusible protein acts in trans - so which lac element is that?

q: A mutant E. coli strain produces beta-galactosidase both in the presence and in the absence of lactose (constitutive expression). Which single mutation could account for this phenotype?
options: An operator mutation that increases binding of the Lac repressor to the operator
A lacI mutation that increases the amount of active Lac repressor produced
An operator mutation that prevents the operator from binding the Lac repressor
A promoter mutation that prevents RNA polymerase from binding the promoter
explain: An operator mutation (O^C) that stops the repressor from binding leaves the operon permanently ON, matching constitutive expression (a lacI mutation making an inactive repressor would do the same). More or tighter-binding repressor would keep the operon OFF, and a promoter mutation blocking RNA polymerase would prevent expression entirely.
hint: Constitutive means the genes are always ON, so the repressor's grip on the operator must be lost. Which mutation stops the repressor from silencing the operon?

q: For the E. coli lac genotype lacI- lacP+ lacO+ lacZ+ lacY- / lacI+ lacP- lacO+ lacZ- lacY+ (chromosome / F' plasmid), with no glucose present, what is the synthesis pattern of beta-galactosidase and permease when lactose is ABSENT versus PRESENT? (+ = synthesized, - = not synthesized)
options: Lactose absent: beta-gal -, permease -; lactose present: beta-gal +, permease +
Lactose absent: beta-gal +, permease +; lactose present: beta-gal +, permease +
Lactose absent: beta-gal -, permease -; lactose present: beta-gal -, permease -
Lactose absent: beta-gal -, permease -; lactose present: beta-gal +, permease -
explain: The lacI+ on the plasmid supplies a diffusible repressor to both operons, so expression is inducible (nothing without lactose). With lactose, only the chromosome has a functional promoter, operator, and lacZ+ together, giving beta-gal; permease is not made because the chromosome's lacY is mutant (Y-) and the plasmid's lacY+ is cis to a dead promoter (P-). Hence lactose present gives beta-gal + but permease -.
hint: First find a functional repressor (either copy provides it, and it acts in trans) - so the system is inducible, not constitutive. Then, for each enzyme, ask whether a working structural gene sits on a copy that also has a working promoter and operator in cis.

q: You are studying an inducible two-gene operon (gene A -> enzyme A, gene B -> enzyme B) that is repressed when inducer xyz is absent and induced when xyz is present. In wild type, both enzymes are OFF without xyz and ON with xyz. Mutant 1 shows: enzyme A OFF and enzyme B ON when xyz is present (still OFF/OFF without xyz). Which single component is mutated in Mutant 1?
options: The operator
Gene A (structural gene for enzyme A)
The promoter
Gene B (structural gene for enzyme B)
explain: Only enzyme A fails to appear even when the operon is properly induced by xyz, while enzyme B is made normally; a mutation confined to the structural gene A explains loss of just that one gene product. An operator or promoter lesion would affect BOTH genes of the operon together, and gene B is clearly intact.
hint: Which enzyme is uniquely lost, and under which condition? A defect that knocks out only one enzyme while the other still responds normally points at that enzyme's own coding sequence.

q: In an inducible operon, a mutation in which single component would MOST directly cause constitutive (always-on) expression of the structural genes?
options: The operator, so the repressor can no longer bind it
The inducer molecule, so it can no longer enter the cell
The operon's own promoter, so RNA polymerase can no longer bind
The operon's structural coding regions, so the enzymes are non-functional
explain: An operator (O-c) mutation destroys the repressor's binding site, so the operon is transcribed constitutively. A promoter knockout would abolish transcription (the opposite effect), a defective inducer or non-functional enzymes would not make the operon constitutively transcribed. (Repressor-gene coding and its promoter can also give constitutive expression, but among these four only the operator does.)
hint: Constitutive means the operon is on even when it shouldn't be. Ask which change would keep the repressor from ever shutting the operon off.

q: In an inducible operon, which mutation would render the repressor protein itself unable to bind the operator, causing constitutive expression?
options: A mutation in the promoter of the repressor gene that blocks synthesis of the repressor
A mutation in the repressor gene that changes the repressor's conformation so it cannot bind the operator
A mutation in the operator that alters the repressor's binding site on the DNA
A mutation in the operon promoter that makes transcription occur constantly
explain: A coding mutation in the repressor gene can give a conformationally altered repressor that cannot bind the operator, so the operon is constitutive. An operator mutation changes the DNA site (not the protein's conformation), and the two promoter options act on transcription levels rather than on the repressor's operator-binding ability.
hint: The question targets a defect in the repressor PROTEIN's own shape, not the DNA site it lands on and not how much repressor gets made.

q: In bacterial genetics, what is a partial diploid (merodiploid)?
options: A bacterium that contains two complete circular chromosomes
A bacterium carrying at least some of the same genes on both its chromosome and a plasmid
An operon considered together with its regulatory protein
A bacterium with one chromosome inherited from its mother and one from its father
explain: A partial diploid is diploid only for the genes duplicated on an F' plasmid plus the chromosome, not for the whole genome. It is not two full chromosomes, not maternal/paternal inheritance (bacteria are haploid), and not an operon-plus-protein complex.
hint: Bacteria are normally haploid. 'Partial' means only some genes are present in two copies, typically because an F' plasmid carries duplicates of chromosomal genes.

q: In lac operon genetics, which of the following alleles is the most dominant, imposing its phenotype even in a partial diploid with wild-type alleles?
options: lacO+, the wild-type operator
lacI-super (lacI^S), the super-repressor
lacI-, the loss-of-function repressor
lacI+, the wild-type repressor
explain: lacI^S encodes a super-repressor that cannot bind inducer, so it stays on every operator in the cell and represses in trans even when wild-type lacI+ is present (dominant to all others, uninducible). lacI- is recessive, and lacO^C is cis-acting so it cannot dominate genes on the other DNA molecule.
hint: Which repressor allele makes a diffusible protein that can't be turned off by inducer? A super-repressor keeps ALL operons in the cell shut down regardless of the other copy.

q: Consider a partial diploid with genotype: chromosome = P+ lacI^S ... P+ lacO+ lacZ+ lacY+ lacA+ ; plasmid = P+ lacI+ ... P- lacO^C lacZ+ lacY+ lacA+ (i.e., a super-repressor lacI^S but otherwise wild-type chromosome, and a plasmid whose operon has a dead promoter P- plus an operator-constitutive lacO^C). What is the result for beta-galactosidase?
options: Beta-galactosidase would not be produced whether or not lactose is present
Beta-galactosidase would be produced only in the absence of lactose
Beta-galactosidase would always be produced whether or not lactose is present
Beta-galactosidase would be produced only in the presence of lactose
explain: The diffusible lacI^S super-repressor cannot be relieved by lactose, so it stays bound to the chromosome's wild-type operator and shuts that operon off in every condition. The plasmid's lacO^C operon escapes the repressor but has a nonfunctional promoter (P-), so it cannot be transcribed either; therefore no beta-galactosidase is made under any condition.
hint: Trace each operon. The super-repressor (lacI^S) is diffusible and can't be induced, so it clamps every functional operator. The plasmid's operon is on lacO^C (repressor can't bind) but its promoter is dead (P-). Can either operon transcribe lacZ?

q: In a eukaryotic cell, dysfunction of which molecule would NOT directly disrupt the process of transcription itself?
options: The template DNA strand
siRNA
A general transcription factor
RNA polymerase II
explain: siRNA mediates post-transcriptional gene silencing (RNAi) and is not part of the machinery that synthesizes RNA, so its loss does not directly halt transcription. RNA polymerase II, general transcription factors, and the template strand are all required to carry out transcription.
hint: Which of these is a post-transcriptional gene-silencing player rather than a core part of the machinery that synthesizes the RNA?

q: Which statement correctly describes the mechanism of RNA interference (RNAi)?
options: Small RNAs add methyl groups directly to mRNA to increase its translation
Small RNAs bind RNA polymerase and prevent it from transcribing any gene
Small RNAs insert themselves into genomic DNA to permanently delete the target gene
Small RNAs base-pair with a complementary mRNA to trigger its degradation or block its translation, and experimentally introduced double-stranded RNA is processed by Dicer to silence a target gene
explain: In RNAi, Dicer cleaves double-stranded RNA into short duplexes; one strand guides a complex to complementary mRNA, causing its degradation or translational repression, which is why introduced dsRNA can knock down a chosen gene. RNAi acts post-transcriptionally on mRNA rather than deleting DNA or globally blocking RNA polymerase.
hint: Focus on what enzyme chops the double-stranded RNA and what the resulting small single strand does when it finds a matching mRNA.

q: Which feature of Caenorhabditis elegans most directly allows researchers to isolate recessive mutations in a single generation?
options: Its predominantly hermaphroditic reproduction, which lets a heterozygote self-fertilize and produce homozygous-recessive offspring
Its transparent body, which lets researchers watch cells directly
Its short generation time, which speeds up crosses
Its fully mapped, invariant cell lineage
explain: Because C. elegans is primarily hermaphroditic, a heterozygous animal can self-fertilize and directly yield homozygous-recessive progeny, exposing recessive phenotypes without an outcross. Transparency, short generation time, and the mapped lineage are all useful traits but do not, by themselves, make a recessive allele homozygous.
hint: A recessive allele only shows a phenotype when homozygous. Which reproductive trait lets a single heterozygous animal generate homozygous offspring by itself?

q: Which small RNA is produced from a hairpin (stem-loop) precursor and regulates gene expression, often by inhibiting translation of target mRNAs?
options: MicroRNA (miRNA)
Ribosomal RNA (rRNA)
Transfer RNA (tRNA)
Small nuclear RNA (snRNA)
explain: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) derive from hairpin RNA precursors and regulate gene expression, frequently by base-pairing with target mRNAs to repress translation. siRNAs, by contrast, arise from double-stranded RNA and usually direct mRNA degradation, which is the classic distractor.
hint: This regulatory RNA is processed from a single-stranded hairpin and typically dampens translation rather than triggering wholesale mRNA cleavage.

q: Which small RNA is generated from double-stranded RNA and binds a complementary mRNA to trigger its degradation?
options: Small interfering RNA (siRNA)
MicroRNA (miRNA)
Small nucleolar RNA (snoRNA)
Transfer RNA (tRNA)
explain: Small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) are processed from double-stranded RNA and guide degradation of complementary mRNA. miRNAs come from single-stranded hairpin precursors and more often repress translation, so it is the tempting but incorrect choice.
hint: Which RNAi molecule comes from a double-stranded precursor and usually leads to cleavage of a perfectly matched target?

q: Which small RNA interacts with Piwi proteins and helps suppress the activity of transposable elements?
options: Piwi-interacting RNA (piRNA)
MicroRNA (miRNA)
Small interfering RNA (siRNA)
Small nuclear RNA (snRNA)
explain: Piwi-interacting RNAs (piRNAs) associate with Piwi-clade Argonaute proteins and silence transposable elements, protecting genome integrity. siRNAs and miRNAs act through the broader Argonaute/RNAi pathways but are not defined by Piwi binding or transposon suppression.
hint: The protein partner is literally in this RNA's name; its main job is guarding the genome (especially the germline) against jumping genes.

q: Which statement best describes RNA interference (RNAi)?
options: A DNA-repair pathway that excises mismatched bases from newly replicated DNA using a small RNA template
The process by which aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases attach amino acids to their cognate tRNAs during translation
A small-RNA-guided process in which short RNAs base-pair with complementary mRNAs, triggering their cleavage/degradation or translational silencing
The addition of a 7-methylguanosine cap to the 5' end of a pre-mRNA to protect it from exonucleases
explain: RNAi is a post-transcriptional silencing mechanism in which small RNAs (siRNA/miRNA) recognize complementary mRNA by base-pairing and direct its cleavage/degradation or block its translation. It is not DNA repair, 5' capping, or tRNA charging — those are unrelated pathways that do not use small-RNA-guided mRNA targeting.
hint: The name tells you two things: it involves RNA, and it interferes with something. Think about what small RNAs like siRNA/miRNA do to a matching message.

q: What is the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC)?
options: A complex of initiation factors and the small ribosomal subunit that scans mRNA for the start codon
The spliceosome, a complex of snRNAs and proteins that removes introns from pre-mRNA
A ribonucleoprotein complex that carries out 5' capping and polyadenylation of pre-mRNA in the nucleus
A protein-RNA complex containing an siRNA or miRNA that uses it as a guide to cleave a target mRNA or repress its translation
explain: RISC is the effector ribonucleoprotein of RNAi: it holds a single-stranded siRNA or miRNA that base-pairs with a complementary mRNA, and the complex then cleaves that mRNA or represses its translation. It is distinct from the capping/polyadenylation machinery, the spliceosome, and the translation-initiation complex, none of which use a small guide RNA to silence a specific transcript.
hint: Break down the name: it is 'RNA-induced' and it causes 'silencing.' What small RNA sits inside it to provide sequence specificity, and what does the complex then do to the matching mRNA?

q: An enzyme called _____ cleaves and processes double-stranded RNA into short fragments to produce siRNAs or miRNAs.
options: Argonaute
aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase
Dicer
RNA polymerase II
explain: Dicer is the ribonuclease that cleaves double-stranded RNA into small siRNA/miRNA duplexes. Argonaute is the catalytic component of the RISC that uses those small RNAs, RNA polymerase II synthesizes RNA rather than dicing it, and aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases charge tRNAs.
hint: The enzyme's name hints at what it does to the double-stranded RNA: it chops it into pieces.

q: In Drosophila tra pre-mRNA, the female-specific splice uses a downstream 3' splice site between exons B and C. Suppose a microRNA (miRNA) or small interfering RNA (siRNA) causes that downstream 3' splice site to be skipped. What is the predicted outcome?
options: The 3' splice site of the intron between exons C and D would always be used, so no female flies form
The 3' splice site of the intron between exons A and B would always be used, so no male flies form
The pre-mRNA would be unable to undergo splicing at all
The 3' splice site between exons B and C would always be used, so no male flies form
explain: If the downstream female-specific 3' splice site is skipped, the transcript defaults to the alternative site between exons C and D, so functional female Tra is never made and no female flies form. Blocking one splice site does not abolish splicing entirely, and it does not eliminate males, since the male default splice is still available.
hint: Making the female-specific splice site unavailable has the same net consequence as deleting it: the transcript defaults to the other splice, eliminating one sex.

q: Genes that encode proteins used in metabolism or biosynthesis (for example, enzymes and structural components of the cell) are called _____ genes.
options: regulatory
promoter
operator
structural
explain: Structural genes encode proteins that carry out metabolism, biosynthesis, and cellular structure. Regulatory genes encode proteins that control expression of other genes, while operator and promoter are cis-acting DNA sites, not genes that encode metabolic proteins.
hint: Contrast genes that code for the working proteins of the cell with those that control other genes.

q: Complete the statement: Trans-acting genes can control expression on _____ DNA molecule(s), while cis-acting genes can control expression on _____ DNA molecule(s).
options: other; the same
other; other
the same; other
the same; the same
explain: Trans-acting genes produce diffusible products (e.g., proteins) that can act on other DNA molecules, whereas cis-acting elements affect only the same DNA molecule on which they reside. This distinction is why a trans-acting repressor can regulate a gene on a separate plasmid, but a cis-acting operator cannot.
hint: Trans-acting products are usually diffusible proteins; cis-acting elements only affect DNA they are physically part of.

q: How do regulatory elements such as promoters and operators affect gene expression?
options: They are translated into proteins that then regulate distant genes.
They diffuse through the cell and can regulate any gene in the genome.
They affect only genes located on a separate DNA molecule.
They affect expression only of genes to which they are physically linked on the same DNA molecule.
explain: Regulatory (cis-acting) elements affect only the genes to which they are physically linked on the same DNA molecule. They are DNA sequences, not diffusible molecules or translated proteins, and they act on their own molecule rather than a separate one.
hint: These are cis-acting DNA sites; think about whether their influence can leave the DNA molecule they sit on.

q: Bacterial genes controlled by the same operon are typically co-regulated because their products are needed together at the same time. Given genes A = defense against bacteria, B = defense against bacteriophages, C = carbohydrate metabolism, D = motility, and E = toxin production, which set is most plausibly organized into a single operon?
options: Genes B, E, and A
Genes C, D, and E
Genes C and D only
Genes A, C, and D
explain: Genes B (defense against bacteriophages), E (toxin production), and A (defense against bacteria) all serve interrelated attack/defense functions that a cell would benefit from expressing together, making them the most plausible single operon. Carbohydrate metabolism and motility serve unrelated purposes and would be regulated separately.
hint: Operons cluster genes whose products serve a shared, coordinated purpose—here, functions tied to attack and defense against other organisms. Which grouping shares that theme?

q: Which statement best describes an operon?
options: A group of unlinked genes each with its own promoter but sharing one repressor
A single gene that is transcribed into multiple different mRNAs by alternative splicing
A region of DNA that encodes only regulatory proteins and no enzymes
A cluster of genes controlled by a single promoter that is transcribed into a single mRNA molecule
explain: An operon is a cluster of adjacent genes transcribed from a single promoter into one (polycistronic) mRNA, allowing coordinated expression. It is not a single alternatively spliced gene, not a regulatory-only region, and its genes are physically linked under one promoter rather than each having its own.
hint: The defining feature is coordinated transcription: several structural genes read off one promoter onto one continuous transcript.

q: A mutation in the operator site of an operon prevents the repressor from binding to it. What is the effect of this mutation on transcription of the structural genes in BOTH a repressible operon and an inducible operon?
options: There will be a significant decrease in the operon's activity
The operon will be constitutively (always) transcriptionally active
It will be impossible to turn on transcription of the structural genes
There will be no change in the operon's activity
explain: In both inducible and repressible operons, the repressor silences transcription by binding the operator; an operator mutation that blocks repressor binding removes the OFF switch, so the operon is transcribed constitutively. It cannot cause a decrease or no change, since the repressor's inhibitory action is lost.
hint: If the repressor can never sit on the operator, nothing can shut the genes off. Consider what happens to transcription when the OFF switch is disabled in both operon types.

q: Two E. coli mutants, huh1 and huh2, each cause constitutive expression of the nop operon (nop1, nop2). One mutant is a point mutation in a DNA-binding site; the other is a deletion of the gene encoding the DNA-binding protein. Given that loss of this protein leads to constitutive (unregulated ON) expression, what is the regulatory role of the DNA-binding protein?
options: A positive regulator (activator)
Cannot be determined from the data
Not a regulator at all
A negative regulator (repressor)
explain: Because deleting the gene for the DNA-binding protein yields constitutive expression, the protein must normally repress the operon, making it a negative regulator. A positive regulator (activator) would be required FOR expression, so its loss would abolish expression rather than make it constitutive.
hint: Deleting the protein leaves the operon stuck ON. If removing a protein makes genes constitutively expressed, was that protein normally turning them on or shutting them off?

q: In Drosophila sex determination via alternative splicing of tra, components are either cis-acting (a DNA/RNA sequence element affecting only its own molecule) or trans-acting (a diffusible protein that can act on separate molecules). Which of the following is correctly classified as trans-acting?
options: The intron between exons B and C
The premature stop codon in the tra transcript
Sxl protein
The alternative 3' splice sites in the tra transcript
explain: Sxl protein (like Tra protein) is a diffusible factor that acts on separate target transcripts, making it trans-acting. Sequence features such as the alternative 3' splice sites and the premature stop codon are cis-acting because they influence only the molecule that contains them.
hint: A diffusible molecule that can travel and act on a different DNA/RNA molecule is trans-acting; a sequence feature that only affects the molecule it sits in is cis-acting.

q: In bacterial gene regulation, how is a 'structural gene' best defined?
options: A DNA sequence that encodes a protein functioning in metabolism or biosynthesis, or serving a structural role in the cell
A gene that is expressed continually without regulation
A DNA sequence encoding a protein or RNA that interacts with other DNA sequences to affect their transcription or translation
A DNA sequence that affects transcription of other DNA sequences to which it is physically linked
explain: A structural gene encodes a protein that does metabolic, biosynthetic, or structural work in the cell. The other choices define a regulatory gene, a regulatory element, and the term 'constitutive,' respectively.
hint: Distinguish the gene that encodes the actual working/building proteins from the genes and sequences that merely control other genes.

q: In an operon, what is the specific role of the 'operator'?
options: It is the group of structural genes transcribed together as a single unit
It is the small molecule that binds a repressor and stimulates transcription in an inducible system
It is the DNA sequence to which a regulator protein binds to control transcription
It is the gene that encodes the regulator protein controlling transcription
explain: The operator is the DNA sequence where a regulator (e.g., repressor) protein binds to control transcription of the operon. The other choices describe the operon itself, the regulator gene, and an inducer, respectively.
hint: The operator is a landing site on the DNA, not a gene or a diffusible molecule. Think about what physically binds there.

q: In the trp operon of E. coli, what happens to the trp repressor in the absence of tryptophan?
options: It cannot bind the operator, so transcription of the trp genes takes place.
It is degraded, permanently preventing transcription of the trp genes.
It binds the promoter and activates transcription of the trp genes.
It binds the operator and blocks transcription of the trp genes.
explain: The trp repressor is inactive without its corepressor (tryptophan), so in the absence of tryptophan it cannot bind the operator and transcription proceeds so the cell can synthesize its own tryptophan. Only when tryptophan is abundant does it act as a corepressor, enabling the repressor to bind the operator and shut the operon off; this is a repressible system, and the repressor is not an activator or degraded.
hint: The trp repressor needs a corepressor to work; identify that corepressor and note it is missing here.

q: In E. coli, tryptophan binds to and activates the trp repressor, which then binds the trp operator/promoter region and blocks RNA polymerase, decreasing transcription of the tryptophan-biosynthesis genes. What type of gene regulation does the trp operon exhibit?
options: Translational regulation
Post-translational protein modification
Positive regulation
Negative regulation
explain: A repressor protein that binds DNA to block RNA polymerase and reduce transcription defines negative regulation. Positive regulation would involve an activator that increases transcription; the control here acts at transcription, not at translation or via protein modification.
hint: The regulatory protein binds DNA to shut transcription OFF. A regulator that turns transcription off is characteristic of which kind of control?

q: The trp operon is under negative regulation by a repressor, and tryptophan is the effector (corepressor) that binds and activates the repressor. Which condition leads to an INCREASE in transcription of the trp operon?
options: The trp repressor is active and bound to the operator
Tryptophan is present at low concentrations, so the repressor cannot bind DNA and dissociates from the operator
Tryptophan binds the repressor, activating it
Tryptophan is present at high concentrations inside the cell
explain: When tryptophan is low, the trp repressor stays inactive and off the operator, so RNA polymerase transcribes the biosynthesis genes - transcription increases. High tryptophan, an active repressor, or Trp binding the repressor all activate repression and decrease transcription.
hint: Tryptophan is the corepressor here. When it is scarce, the repressor stays inactive and off the DNA - so what happens to transcription?

q: Consider the negative-feedback cycle of the trp operon, starting from a state where tryptophan levels in the cell are LOW and ending when transcription of the genes stops. Which step comes FIRST in this sequence?
options: RNA polymerase binds the promoter, allowing transcription of the trp genes to proceed
The trp repressor binds the operator
Tryptophan binds the trp repressor and induces a conformational change
The trp repressor blocks RNA polymerase from binding the promoter
explain: With tryptophan low, the repressor is inactive and the operator is open, so the cycle begins with RNA polymerase binding the promoter and transcribing the trp genes. Trp binding the repressor, the repressor binding the operator, and blocking RNA polymerase are all later steps that occur only after tryptophan accumulates.
hint: Start where tryptophan is scarce: the repressor is inactive, so the operator is free. What can RNA polymerase do at that moment?

q: In the trp operon, attenuation depends on RNA leader regions that can form alternative hairpins; region 3 pairs with region 4 to form the transcription-terminator hairpin. What is predicted if there is a large deletion of region 3 of the trp leader?
options: The operon will be transcribed but the structural genes will not be translated
The structural genes will never be transcribed at any tryptophan level
The structural genes will be transcribed only when tryptophan levels are low
The entire trp operon will be transcribed regardless of the cellular tryptophan level
explain: Deleting region 3 prevents formation of the 3-4 terminator hairpin, so attenuation cannot occur and the operon is transcribed constitutively regardless of tryptophan. Tryptophan-dependent termination requires region 3, so low-Trp-only and never-transcribed outcomes are wrong.
hint: The terminator hairpin needs region 3 to pair with region 4. If region 3 is gone, that terminator can't form, so RNA polymerase is never told to stop early.

q: trp operon attenuation works because the ribosome translating the leader stalls at tandem Trp codons when tryptophan is scarce, changing which RNA hairpin forms. What is predicted if the Trp codons in the leader peptide are replaced by codons for isoleucine?
options: The structural genes will be transcribed only when isoleucine levels are low
The operon will be transcribed but the structural genes will not be translated
The structural genes will never be transcribed
The structural genes will be transcribed only when tryptophan levels are low
explain: Replacing the Trp codons with Ile codons makes the ribosome stall on scarce isoleucine instead, so the antiterminator forms and the structural genes are transcribed only when isoleucine is low. The operon now responds to Ile, not Trp, so the tryptophan-dependent and never/always answers are wrong.
hint: Attenuation senses whichever amino acid the leader's control codons call for, via ribosome stalling. Swap the sensed codons and you swap which amino acid's scarcity turns the operon on.

q: Attenuation of the trp operon requires a ribosome to translate the leader RNA while it is still being transcribed. Why is attenuation common in prokaryotes but essentially absent in eukaryotes?
options: Only prokaryotes can form hairpin (stem-loop) structures in RNA
Eukaryotic ribosomes translate too fast for attenuation to work
In eukaryotes, transcription and translation usually occur in separate physical locations
Only prokaryotes are able to synthesize amino acids
explain: Attenuation depends on coupled transcription-translation, which needs the ribosome and RNA polymerase on the same mRNA simultaneously; in eukaryotes transcription (nucleus) and translation (cytoplasm) are physically separated, so a ribosome cannot influence a still-elongating transcript. Both cell types make amino acids and form RNA hairpins, and eukaryotic ribosome speed is not the barrier.
hint: Attenuation needs a ribosome reading the mRNA at the same moment RNA polymerase is still making it. What compartment barrier in eukaryotes makes that coupling impossible?
